entry.title: How We Built a Scalable Mass Hiring Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM Using SAP BTP
entry.content:
Introduction

In many organizations using SAP SuccessFactors HCM, hiring doesn’t always happen at a steady pace. Sometimes it comes in waves — seasonal hiring, rapid expansion, new project launches — and suddenly HR teams are expected to onboard large numbers of employees in a very short time.

From what I’ve personally experienced, this is when traditional hiring processes start to feel overwhelming.

I’ve seen HR teams juggling spreadsheets, manually entering data, coordinating approvals over emails, and constantly double-checking records to avoid mistakes. For a few hires, this might be manageable. But when the numbers grow, the process becomes stressful and error-prone. Small inconsistencies in pre-hire records can cause issues later in onboarding. Delayed approvals slow everything down. And HR teams end up spending more time fixing data than focusing on people.

What really stood out to me was how exhausting and reactive the process became during high-volume hiring drives. Everyone was working hard — but the system wasn’t working smart.

At the same time, simply trying to “move faster” without structure creates new risks. Speed without governance can impact compliance, reporting accuracy, and overall data integrity. So the real challenge wasn’t just hiring faster — it was hiring smarter.

That’s where the idea for a more streamlined and intelligent solution began.

Our Approach

To address these challenges, we built the Mass and Swift Hiring Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

While working on this solution, my focus was simple: reduce manual effort without losing control. I wanted to create something that HR teams would actually enjoy using — something that feels familiar, intuitive, and aligned with how SuccessFactors already works.

The application provides a clean and structured interface where HR users can manage employee hiring, initiate onboarding for pre-hires, and handle contingent worker hiring in a guided and automated way. Instead of jumping between systems or manually validating data, the process becomes smooth and controlled.

One area I paid special attention to was former employee rehiring. In real-world scenarios, this can get complicated. If not handled correctly, it can disrupt employee history and create reporting inconsistencies. So the solution intelligently detects rehire cases and ensures the correct hiring flow is followed — preserving data integrity while simplifying the process for HR.

Most importantly, automation here doesn’t mean losing governance. Every hiring action still respects validations, approvals, audit requirements, and compliance standards. The difference is that the system now does the heavy lifting.

With this approach, organizations can handle bulk or time-sensitive hiring with much more confidence and far less operational stress.

Key Features and Benefits

1. User- Friendly Hiring Interface

  • SAP Fiori-based responsive UI.
  • Guided steps for data entry, validation, and submission.
  • Supports desktop and mobile access via SAP Build Work Zone.

 

2. Rapid Mass Hiring

  • Create and process multiple hires in a single operation.
  • Preserves and significantly reduces hiring cycle time for bulk recruitment.
  • Ideal for graduate hiring, seasonal hiring, and high-volume recruitment drives.

 

3. Rehire Detection Using Core Employee Data

  • The application performs an automatic rehire check using the candidate’s First Name, Last Name, and Date of Birth, National ID and other attributes.
  • If an existing record is found in SAP SuccessFactors HCM with an inactive status, the system recognizes the individual as a rehire and continues with the rehire process instead of creating a new hire.
  • This helps avoid duplicate employee records and ensures continuity of employee data.

4. Download Standard Hiring Template

  • Provides a predefined, SuccessFactors-aligned template for bulk hiring data entry.
  • Ensures users follow the correct format, mandatory fields, and data structure.
  • Can be easily shared with recruitment partners or external agencies.

 

5. Import Data

  • Allows HR users to import data using structured files (CSV / JSON / XML).
  • Eliminates manual data entry for large hiring volumes.
  • Also allows HR users to manually create a single record directly from the application UI.
  • Supports validation of uploaded data before submission to SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Handles errors gracefully by highlighting invalid or missing fields.

6. Data Validation with Error Highlighting and Logs

  • Performs real-time and pre-processing validation on imported hiring data before submission to SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Validates mandatory fields, data formats, and business rules aligned with SuccessFactors requirements.
  • Highlights validation errors clearly at row and field level, making it easy for users to identify and correct issues.
  • Prevents partial or incorrect data from being processed, ensuring data integrity.

 

7. Download Validation Logs

  • Provides the ability to download detailed error logs for failed records.
  • Error logs include clear reasons for failure (e.g., missing mandatory fields, invalid values).
  • Enables quick correction and re-upload of only failed records.

 

8. Download Successfully Imported Records

  • Successfully imported records can be downloadable.
  • Users can download the list of successfully imported records for confirmation, and audit.
  • Provides full transparency on which records were created successfully in SAP SuccessFactors.

 

9. Create New Onboardee from Scratch

  • Enables HR users to create a new onboardee directly from the application, without relying on pre-hire data.
  • Supports direct entry of onboardee information through a validated UI.
  • Ideal for walk-in hires, urgent hires, contractor conversions, or scenarios where pre-hire data is not available.
  • Ensures all mandatory SuccessFactors fields are captured before submission.
  • Seamlessly creates onboarding records in SAP SuccessFactors using secure OData APIs.

 

10. Simplified Contingent Worker Hiring

  • Enables quick and bulk creation of contingent workers alongside regular hires.
  • Minimizes manual intervention and repetitive HR tasks.
  • Ensures contingent worker data is validated and created consistently in SAP SuccessFactors.

 

Business Value

  • The solution enables organizations to significantly reduce hiring cycle times during high-volume recruitment by streamlining data capture, validations, and record creation for employees and contingent workers.
  • Built-in validations ensure that only complete and consistent data is processed, reducing errors in pre-hire, employee and contingent records and ensuring alignment with SAP SuccessFactors HCM business rules.
  • By simplifying and automating repetitive hiring activities, HR teams can spend less time on transactional work and focus more on strategic workforce planning and talent engagement.
  • Detailed success and failure logs provide clear visibility into hiring outcomes, supporting audits, reconciliation activities, and operational reporting without additional manual effort.
  • The solution supports both full-time and contingent worker hiring through a single framework, allowing organizations to adapt quickly to changing workforce demands.

Conclusion

High-volume hiring can quickly turn into a pressure test for HR teams and systems. I’ve seen how manual processes, fragmented steps, and constant follow-ups can slow down even the most capable teams.

The Mass and Swift Hiring Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM helps remove that pressure by bringing structure, automation, and reliability into the process. It allows organizations to scale hiring without sacrificing data quality, compliance, or control.

For me, this solution is not just about speeding up recruitment. It’s about giving HR teams breathing space — allowing them to focus on people, strategy, and growth instead of repetitive operational tasks.

When hiring becomes both fast and reliable, organizations don’t just fill positions quickly — they build their workforce with confidence.

entry.title: Custom Handlers for Entities in SAP CAP
entry.content:
The first time I faced a two-level composition hierarchy with draft-enabled entities in a CAP project, I stared at my screen wondering which custom handlers I actually needed to implement. The Fiori Elements UI works beautifully out of the box—users can create, edit, save, and discard changes without any custom code. But the moment you need business logic, validations, or calculations, you realize you’re dancing with an intricate system of events that needs careful choreography. Nowadays, while shifting from one project to another or after sometime without 'coding'. I'm wondering which  is the correct one that I have to use. For that reason I created this blog post to helps me surf between the events and their lifecycle in draft entities.

In this post, I’ll walk through each Fiori Elements action and explain exactly what custom handlers you need to implement for a draft-enabled entity with nested compositions. My mind loves finding patterns, and CAP’s draft system is essentially a beautiful pattern that becomes clearer once you understand the rhythm.

Understanding the Scenario

Let’s establish our data model first. We have:

  • Entity A: Draft-enabled parent entity
  • Entity B: Composition of Entity A (automatically draft-enabled)

Here’s the critical insight: when a parent entity is draft-enabled, all compositions in the hierarchy automatically inherit draft behavior. There’s no such thing as mixing draft and non-draft entities in a composition tree—CAP handles the entire hierarchy as one cohesive draft unit.

This means when a user edits Entity A, CAP creates draft copies of all related Entity B records. When they activate (save) the draft, all changes across the hierarchy are committed atomically. This transactional behavior is what makes the draft system powerful, but it also means your custom handlers need to respect this hierarchy.

Entity A (draft-enabled)
  └── Entity B[] (composition, inherits draft)

The Fiori Elements Action Lifecycle

When users interact with a Fiori Elements application, their actions trigger specific CAP events. Let’s break down each action, the events it triggers, and the custom handlers you need.

Quick Reference Matrix

User ActionCAP EventTarget EntityEntities AffectedWhen To use
Create (New)NEWEntityA.drafts, EntityB.draftsEntityA draft; EntityB when adding a child rowSet defaults, initialize structure
Edit (Existing)EDITEntityAAll entities (A, B) - active → draft copyValidate edit allowed, enrich draft context
Update (Modify fields)PATCHEntityA.drafts, EntityB.draftsSpecific draft entity being changedField validation, calculations, cascade updates
Save/Activate

SAVE then 

CREATE/UPDATE

EntityA.drafts → EntityAAll entities (draft → active)Critical: Validate all entities, business logic
DeleteDELETE

EntityA or EntityA.drafts; 

EntityB.drafts for individual child rows

All entities via cascade (root delete); specific child rowPrevent deletion, cleanup resources
Cancel/DiscardDISCARDEntityA.draftsAll draft entities deletedCleanup temp resources

Important Notes:

  • SAVE is special: It triggers BOTH a SAVE event on drafts AND either CREATE (for new entities) or UPDATE (for edited entities) on the active entity. You need handlers for all three.
  • Compositions inherit draft: When EntityA is draft-enabled, EntityB automatically becomes draft-enabled. No way to mix draft/non-draft in a composition tree.
  • Deep operations: CAP handles the entire hierarchy automatically during EDIT and SAVE. Trust it unless you have specific needs.
  • Use .drafts qualifier: Always distinguish between EntityA (active) and EntityA.drafts (draft) in your handlers.

Action 1: NEW (Creating a New Draft)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Create” button in the list report, or adds a new row to a composition child table
  • CAP Event: NEW on EntityA.drafts (new parent draft) or NEW on EntityB.drafts (new child row added during editing)
  • Entities Affected: EntityA draft when creating; EntityB draft when the user adds a row to the composition table
  • When You Need a Handler: To set default values, initialize related entities, or prepare the initial structure
import { Request } from '@sap/cds';

// Fires when the user clicks "Create" in the list report
srv.before('NEW', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Set default values for the new draft
  req.data.status = 'NEW';
  req.data.createdBy = req.user.id;
});

srv.after('NEW', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Enrich the created draft with calculated fields
  // or fetch additional data to display
  const { ID } = data;

  // Example: Calculate some initial values based on user context
  await UPDATE('EntityA.drafts')
    .set({ calculatedField: someCalculation() })
    .where({ ID });
});

// Fires when the user adds a new row to the EntityB composition table
// This is independent from the parent NEW — it fires on the child entity directly
srv.before('NEW', 'EntityB.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Set defaults for the new child row
  req.data.position = await getNextPosition(req.data.parent_ID);
  req.data.lineStatus = 'OPEN';
});

srv.after('NEW', 'EntityB.drafts', async (data: EntityB, req: Request) => {
  // Post-process the newly created child row
  const { ID } = data;
  await UPDATE('EntityB.drafts')
    .set({ computedField: computeDefault(data) })
    .where({ ID });
});

Action 2: EDIT (Editing an Active Entity)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Edit” button on an existing record
  • CAP Event: EDIT action on EntityA, which internally triggers draft creation
  • Entities Affected: All entities in the hierarchy (active → draft copy)
  • When You Need a Handler: To customize the draft preparation, load additional context, or handle special composition copying logic
srv.before('EDIT', 'EntityA', async (req: Request) => {
  // Fetch the active entity before it's copied to draft
  const { ID } = req.data;
  const entity = await SELECT.one.from('EntityA').where({ ID });

  // You can perform validations before allowing edit
  if (entity.locked) {
    req.error(423, 'This entity is locked and cannot be edited');
  }
});

srv.after('EDIT', 'EntityA', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // The draft has been created with deep copy of all compositions
  // You can now enrich the draft with additional data
  const { ID } = data;

  // Example: Load some context data into the draft
  await UPDATE('EntityA.drafts')
    .set({ editContext: 'User edited at ' + new Date().toISOString() })
    .where({ ID });

  // CAP automatically handles the deep copy of EntityB
  // You typically don't need to manually copy compositions
});

Action 3: PATCH (Modifying a Draft)

  • User Action: Changes field values while editing (each field change triggers this)
  • CAP Event: PATCH on EntityA.drafts or EntityB.drafts
  • Entities Affected: The specific draft entity being modified
  • When You Need a Handler: For field-level validation, calculated fields, or cascading updates
srv.before('PATCH', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Validate the changes before they're applied
  const { amount, currency } = req.data;

  if (amount && amount < 0) {
    req.error(400, 'Amount must be positive');
  }

  // Perform calculations based on changed fields
  if (req.data.quantity && req.data.pricePerUnit) {
    req.data.totalPrice = req.data.quantity * req.data.pricePerUnit;
  }
});

srv.after('PATCH', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Cascade updates to child entities if needed
  const { ID } = data;

  // Example: When parent changes, update all children
  if (req.data.status) {
    await UPDATE('EntityB.drafts')
      .set({ parentStatus: req.data.status })
      .where({ parent_ID: ID });
  }
});

// Handle updates on child entities
srv.before('PATCH', 'EntityB.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Validate child entity changes
  // These fire independently when users edit composition tables
  if (req.data.invalidField) {
    req.error(400, 'Invalid field value');
  }
});

Action 4: SAVE/Activate (Persisting Draft Changes)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Save” button
  • CAP Event: SAVE on EntityA.drafts, which triggers either CREATE or UPDATE on the active EntityA
  • Entities Affected: All entities in the hierarchy (draft → active)
  • When You Need a Handler: This is where most business logic lives—validation, computation, and composition management
// IMPORTANT: The SAVE handler runs on the draft entity
srv.before('SAVE', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // This is your last chance to validate before activation
  const draft = await SELECT.one
    .from('EntityA.drafts')
    .where({ ID: req.data.ID })
    .columns(['ID', 'requiredField', 'status']);

  if (!draft.requiredField) {
    req.error(400, 'Required field is missing');
  }

  // Validate all child entities in the composition
  const childrenB: EntityB[] = await SELECT.from('EntityB.drafts')
    .where({ parent_ID: draft.ID });

  for (const childB of childrenB) {
    if (!childB.mandatoryField) {
      req.error(400, `EntityB ${childB.ID} is missing mandatory field`);
    }
  }
});

// Handle new entity creation (when activating a draft created via "Create")
srv.on('CREATE', 'EntityA', async (req: Request, next: Function) => {
  // The draft is being activated and will become a new active entity
  // CAP handles the deep insert of all compositions automatically

  // But you might need to customize the creation
  const result = await next(); // Let CAP do the default deep insert

  // Post-creation logic
  await postProcessNewEntity(result.ID);

  return result;
});

// Handle entity update (when activating a draft created via "Edit")
srv.on('UPDATE', 'EntityA', async (req: Request, next: Function) => {
  // The draft changes are being applied to the active entity
  // CAP handles the deep update of all compositions

  // You can intercept to add custom logic
  const { ID } = req.data;

  // CAP's deep update uses a "full set" approach:
  // - Existing child records not in the draft are deleted
  // - New child records in the draft are created
  // - Modified child records are updated

  const result = await next(); // Let CAP handle the deep update

  // Post-update logic
  await notifyRelatedSystems(ID);

  return result;
});

srv.after('SAVE', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // The draft has been successfully activated
  // This fires AFTER the CREATE or UPDATE completes

  // Good place for notifications, logging, or async processes
  await sendNotification({
    message: 'Entity A has been saved',
    entityId: data.ID
  });
});

Action 5: DELETE (Removing an Entity)

  • User Action: Deletes a draft or active record
  • CAP Event: DELETE on EntityA or EntityA.drafts
  • Entities Affected: All entities in the hierarchy via cascade deletion
  • When You Need a Handler: To prevent deletion under certain conditions, clean up related data, or handle cascade manually
srv.before('DELETE', 'EntityA', async (req: Request) => {
  // Validate deletion is allowed
  const { ID } = req.data;
  const entity = await SELECT.one.from('EntityA').where({ ID });

  if (entity.status === 'LOCKED') {
    req.error(403, 'Cannot delete locked entities');
  }

  // CAP will automatically cascade delete to EntityB
  // due to the composition relationship

  // But you might want to clean up related data outside the composition
  await deleteRelatedResources(ID);
});

srv.after('DELETE', 'EntityA', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Entity and all compositions have been deleted
  // Clean up, log, or notify
  await logDeletion(data.ID, req.user.id);
});

// Deleting the entire draft (when user deletes the whole unsaved record)
srv.before('DELETE', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Usually no custom logic needed here
  // CAP handles cascade deletion of draft compositions
  // But you might want to log or clean up
});

// Fires when the user removes an individual row from the EntityB composition table
// This is different from deleting the whole parent draft — it targets a specific child row
srv.before('DELETE', 'EntityB.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  const { ID } = req.data;
  const item = await SELECT.one.from('EntityB.drafts').where({ ID });

  if (item.protected) {
    req.error(403, `Item ${item.ID} cannot be removed`);
  }
});

srv.after('DELETE', 'EntityB.drafts', async (data: EntityB, req: Request) => {
  // Child row has been removed from the draft composition
  // You might want to recalculate totals or reorder positions on the parent draft
  await recalculateTotals(data.parent_ID);
});

Action 6: CANCEL (Discarding Draft Changes)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Cancel” or “Discard Draft” button
  • CAP Event: Draft discard action
  • Entities Affected: All draft entities in the hierarchy are deleted
  • When You Need a Handler: To clean up temporary data or resources created during draft editing
srv.before('DISCARD', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Clean up any temporary resources or cached data
  const { ID } = req.data;

  // Example: Delete temporary files uploaded to the draft
  await cleanupTemporaryFiles(ID);

  // CAP will automatically delete all draft entities in the composition
});

srv.after('DISCARD', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Draft has been discarded
  // Log the discard action if needed
  await logDraftDiscard(data.ID, req.user.id);
});

Key Patterns & Best Practices

After working with draft-enabled compositions, here are the patterns I’ve found most useful:

1. Use the .drafts qualifier consistently: Always be explicit about whether you’re handling drafts or active entities. EntityA.drafts vs EntityA are different entities with different behavior.

2. Handle both CREATE and UPDATE during SAVE: When a draft is activated, it triggers either CREATE (new entity) or UPDATE (edited entity). You need handlers for both scenarios—they’re not interchangeable.

3. Trust CAP’s deep operations: CAP automatically handles deep INSERT and UPDATE for your entire composition hierarchy. Don’t try to manually manage child entities during activation unless you have specific requirements.

4. Validate in before(‘SAVE’): The SAVE event is your last checkpoint before draft activation. Put comprehensive validation here, including validation of child and grandchild entities.

5. Manage transactions carefully: All operations during draft activation are transactional. If you throw an error or call req.error(), the entire activation (including all compositions) will roll back.

6. Use after handlers for side effects: Send notifications, update external systems, or trigger async processes in after handlers. These run only after successful completion, ensuring consistency.

 

Rules for AI Code Assistants (CAP Draft)

Use this a template for your rules.

# SAP CAP Draft Handler Events

## Draft root entity (EntityA.drafts)
- Create new draft    → NEW       (not CREATE)
- Edit field in draft → PATCH     (not UPDATE)
- Save/activate draft → SAVE → then CREATE (new) or UPDATE (edit) on active entity
- Edit existing       → EDIT      on active entity (not drafts)
- Discard draft       → DISCARD   (not discard)
- Delete              → DELETE    on EntityA or EntityA.drafts

## Non-draft entities — standard CRUD only
- CREATE / READ / UPDATE / DELETE on the entity directly
- No .drafts qualifier, no NEW/PATCH/SAVE/EDIT/DISCARD

## Key rules
- Always use .drafts qualifier to distinguish draft from active entity
- SAVE handlers go on EntityA.drafts; activate handlers on active EntityA
- before('SAVE') is the last validation checkpoint before activation
- after() handlers are for side effects (notifications, external calls)
- All activation operations are transactional; req.error() rolls back everything

In case that  the files are very big think to add as an external reference as: 

## To choose event and target entity for a Custom Handler for entity check `<relative_project_root>/<code_assistant>/docs/cap_handlers.md`

This documentation is based in amazing job in https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/node.js/fiori

entry.title: When Business Asks for a Bicycle, Don't Build a Spaceship
entry.content:
When Business Asks for a Bicycle, Don't Build a Spaceship

I have developed a comprehensive playbook demonstrating how modern SAP tools and AI can deliver solutions in DAYS instead of MONTHS.

The shocking math:
- Traditional approach: 6 months, $650K in delayed value
- SAP tools + AI: 1 week, $25K in delayed value

This isn't just theory—it's a practical guide that covers:
- CDS Views for data modeling
- CAP for service generation
- Fiori Elements for instant UIs
- Integration Suite for connectivity
- AI to accelerate everything

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/rajesh-putumbaka-456267101_sap-btp-ai-development-playbook-ugcPost-7431029390173696001-y7Yn?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAABnYP8IBvlY4GDRmR8utVFLHXZ61gUvSa-0 
Download the full playbook (PDF attached) and share your thoughts: Have you encountered the "perfect is the enemy of good" trap in your SAP projects?
#SAP #DigitalTransformation #AI #SAPCommunity

entry.title: Gregor Wolf
entry.content:
About Gregor

  • Germany | SAP Mentor since 2007 | Follow Gregor on LinkedIn

I am an independent SAP solution architect, developer and SAP Mentor. I focus on SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), Node.JS, SAP Fiori Elements and Security. I've supported several customers during their SAP Cloud ERP Private Edition (S/4HANA On Premise) implementation. Since the end of 2024 I support the technical topics at an SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition Customer.

Topics of interest: SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), SAP Cloud ERP public and private, SAPUI5, OpenUI5, SAP Fiori, SAP HANA Cloud

Gregor, what inspired you to become an SAP Mentor?

A key influence early in my SAP Community journey was Mark Finnern. He is the father of the SAP Community. Mark encouraged me to move beyond answering questions and start sharing detailed technical how-to's but also event reports in blog posts. In 2007 Mark also founded the SAP Mentors program which gave me the opportunity to provide structured, experience-based feedback to SAP product teams and help bridge the gap between product vision and real-world implementation.

What advice would like to share with other SAP community members?

My advice to other SAP Community members is simple: contribute consistently and think long-term.

Start by answering questions and helping others solve real problems - but don’t stop there. Document your lessons learned, share architectural insights, and write about what worked and what didn’t. That’s how collective expertise grows. And you will be amazed how often you research years later and find your own answers and blog posts to solve a problem you're just facing. Despite all the new stuff SAP has quite a long tail that doesn't change quickly.

Engage constructively, challenge respectfully, and focus on signal over noise. The community is not just a support forum - it’s an ecosystem where practitioners can influence product direction, elevate standards, and shape the future of SAP together.

entry.title: Part 1: Creating and Deploying a SAP CAP Application with SAP HANA on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry)
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the First Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone

 

Introduction

SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is often demonstrated through local examples, but its real value becomes clear when the application is deployed to SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and backed by a managed database.

In this blog, we will go through creating a basic SAP CAP application using the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) CAP template, binding it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deploying it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime.

The goal here is not to build a complex application, but to establish a clean, working reference point. I start with a minimal CAP project to keep the focus on deployment rather than domain complexity.

This blog serves as a baseline for a series where the same CAP application will later be deployed using different databases and runtimes. The focus here is not application complexity, but establishing a clean, working reference point.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • Active Hana Cloud Instance
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

 

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a CAP Project from Template given in the BAS:

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-hana-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: SAP HANA Cloud
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

 

CAP Project creation wizard with SAP HANA selected

 

This will create a CAP project for you with mta.yaml automatically updated. The generated project follows standard CAP structure:

  • app –  UI
  • db – Schema and data files
  • srv – Service Definition

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Development:

Follow the following steps:

  • Define a Simple Data Model:

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;
entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

               

  • Expose the Model via CAP Service:

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

  • Add Data:

Run the following in the project terminal:

cds add data --records 5

 

  • Run the Application locally:

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build
cds deploy

 

  • Add a Managed Approuter:
  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

 

This is important to view the data once deployed.

 

Prepare the Project for Deployment:

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

 

Build the CAP Project:

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application:

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry deployment

 

During deployment:

  • HDI Container is created
  • Database schema is deployed
  • CAP Service is bound to the database automatically

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application:

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

With that, we have successfully created a minimal SAP CAP application, connected it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime. While the application itself is intentionally simple, the deployment pipeline we established here — from schema definition to MTA build to Cloud Foundry deployment — reflects a production-ready workflow that scales well as complexity grows.

The key takeaways from this exercise are that the BAS CAP template significantly reduces boilerplate by auto-generating the mta.yaml with the necessary modules and bindings, the HDI container handles schema deployment seamlessly, and CAP's convention-over-configuration approach means your OData service is ready without any manual REST wiring.

This baseline will serve as the foundation for the rest of this series, where we will explore deploying the same application with different databases, runtimes, and configurations — making it easier to understand the trade-offs of each approach. Stay tuned for the next part!

 

Next Steps

In the upcoming blogs, we will focus on creating the same application using a PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option.

entry.title: Avoiding HANA Connection Exhaustion: A Practical Guide to Hikari & Multi-Tenant Scaling
entry.content:
Introduction

Modern SAP applications running on SAP BTP with CAP Java and SAP HANA rely heavily on efficient database connection management.

Opening a new database connection for every request is expensive:

  • It increases latency
  • It consumes database resources
  • It reduces scalability

To solve this, we use connection pools.

In this blog, we’ll walk through how connection pooling works in CAP Java, what can go wrong in multi-tenant systems, and how to configure it correctly to avoid production outages.

What is a Connection Pool?

A connection pool is a cache of reusable database connections maintained by your application.

open connection → execute query → close connection

becomes:

borrow connection → execute query → return to pool

Benefits

  • Faster response times
  • Reduced database load
  • Reuse of expensive DB sessions
  • Better scalability

How CAP Java Uses Connection Pools

In CAP Java:

  • Database access uses JDBC connection pooling via Spring Boot
  • The default pool implementation is HikariCP
  • Each application instance maintains its own connection pool

This means that if your application is scaled horizontally, the number of connections increases with the number of instances.

Why This Becomes Critical in Production

In the basic mode, the HANA database is provisioned with 2 vCPUs, which allows roughly 1000 concurrent connections.

Now consider a typical CAP multi-tenant setup:

  • Multiple services
  • Multiple tenants
  • Multiple app instances (autoscaling)
  • Each instance maintaining its own pool

The total DB connections can grow very quickly if not controlled.

Multi-Tenant Scaling Problem

Without optimization, the connection count grows like this:

connections = instances × tenants × pool size

Example:

  • 4 tenants
  • 3 application instances
  • default pool size = 10
4 × 3 × 10 = 120 connections

Now multiply this across multiple services and sidecars, you can quickly reach the HANA limit.

The Solution: Shared Pools with combinePools

CAP Java provides a very important optimization:

cds:
  multi-tenancy:
    datasource:
      combinePools:
        enabled: true

This ensures that instead of creating pools per tenant, the application uses:

One shared connection pool per HANA database per application instance

Now the formula becomes:

connections = instances × pool size

This makes connection usage predictable and scalable.

HikariCP Configuration (Production)

A production-ready CAP Java configuration looks like this:

cds:
  multi-tenancy:
    datasource:
      combinePools:
        enabled: true
      hikari:
        maximum-pool-size: 40
        minimum-idle: 10
        idle-timeout: 600000
        max-lifetime: 3300000

Parameter Description

maximumPoolSizeMaximum connections per instance
minimumIdleWarm connections kept ready
idleTimeoutIdle connection eviction time
maxLifetimeMaximum lifetime before connection refresh

How to Size Your Pool Correctly

Use this simple guideline:

maximumPoolSize ≈ concurrent DB requests per instance

And always validate:

instances × maximumPoolSize < HANA DB connection limit

For example:

  • 3 instances
  • pool size 40
3 × 40 = 120 connections

Well within a 1000 connection limit.

Monitoring & Observability

You should continuously monitor:

  • Active DB connections
  • Idle connections
  • Pool utilization
  • Total HANA connection usage

Set alerts at 70–80% of DB capacity.

Common Pitfalls

  • Not enabling combinePools in multi-tenant apps
  • Oversizing connection pools
  • Multiple services sharing same HANA DB without coordination
  • No monitoring of DB connection usage
  • Assuming default values are safe for production

Summary

Area Recommendation

Connection PoolUse HikariCP
Multi-TenancyEnable combinePools
Pool SizingBased on concurrency
MonitoringMandatory
ScalingValidate against DB limits

Final Thoughts

Connection pooling is often invisible, until it fails in production.

With the right configuration in CAP Java and SAP HANA, you can:

  • avoid outages
  • improve performance
  • scale safely in multi-tenant environments

If you are running CAP Java in production, take a few minutes to review your pool configuration, it can prevent your next incident.

References

  • HikariCP
  • DB Connection Pooling
  • CAP Datasource Configuration

entry.title: The Hidden Cost of Custom Code in SAP IS U — And How BTP Reduces It Without a Big Bang
entry.content:
Executive Summary

For years, SAP IS‑U has quietly carried the weight of utility operations. Billing accuracy, regulatory compliance, and meter‑to‑cash stability always came first — and rightly so. But the side effect of that stability is a landscape full of Z‑programs, user exits, and enhancements that were built for good reasons at the time, yet now create friction when utilities try to modernize.

As the industry shifts toward decarbonization, DER growth, and real‑time customer expectations, the question isn’t “Is custom code a problem?” It’s “How do we reduce its impact without breaking the systems that keep the lights on?”

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) offers a practical, low‑risk way to separate custom logic from the core and prepare for S/4HANA — without a disruptive rewrite or a multi‑year freeze.

1. The Custom Code Reality Inside SAP IS‑U

 Most custom code in IS‑U wasn’t a mistake. It was a necessity.

Utilities added enhancements to:

  • Meet state‑specific regulatory rules
  • Support complex rate structures
  • Integrate with legacy CIS, AMI, and market systems
  • Automate workflows long before APIs or event frameworks existed

Over time, this created:

  • Thousands of Z‑objects
  • Enhancements buried deep inside billing, device management, and FICA
  • Business logic tightly coupled to SAP’s upgrade cycle

What once delivered flexibility now slows down change.

2. The Hidden Costs Most Programs Underestimate

2.1 Upgrade and Migration Friction

Every enhancement inside the core increases:

  • SP and EHP regression testing effort
  • S/4HANA conversion complexity
  • Dependency on niche IS‑U technical expertise

Custom code does not just slow upgrades — it dictates upgrade timing.

2.2 Operational Risk

Many utilities still rely on:

  • Poorly documented enhancements
  • Knowledge concentrated in a few long‑tenured developers
  • Custom logic triggered deep inside critical transactions

This creates silent operational risk — especially during incident response or regulatory audits.

2.3 Innovation Tax

Each new digital initiative must answer an uncomfortable question:

“Do we modify the core again, or build around it?”

When the core is overloaded with custom logic, innovation slows — not because ideas are weak, but because change becomes expensive and risky.

3. Why ‘Rewrite Everything’ Is Not a Strategy

Most utilities cannot absorb:

  • A full custom code rewrite
  • A large‑scale CIS replacement
  • A multi‑year freeze on business change

A big‑bang approach often introduces more risk than it removes.

A more realistic path is selective decoupling — and that’s where BTP fits naturally.

4. How SAP BTP Reduces Custom Code Impact — Pragmatically

BTP gives utilities a way to shift custom logic out of the core while keeping IS‑U stable.

4.1 Keep the Core Clean — for Real

Instead of embedding logic in IS‑U:

  • Business rules move to BTP services
  • Extensions run side‑by‑side, not inside ECC or S/4HANA
  • Core processes remain upgrade‑friendly

4.2 Event‑Driven Extensions

Using events and APIs:

  • IS‑U triggers events (billing completion, device updates, move‑in/out)
  • BTP processes logic asynchronously
  • Failures are isolated, not system‑wide

4.3 Reusable Innovation Layer

Once on BTP, the same extension framework can support:

  • DER orchestration
  • AI‑based billing analytics
  • Customer self‑service enhancements
  • Regulatory reporting automation

This turns custom logic into assets, not liabilities.

5. Reference Architecture (Conceptual)

Below is a simplified conceptual architecture illustrating how SAP IS‑U and BTP coexist.

This model allows utilities to:

  • Retire high‑risk custom code gradually
  • Introduce new capabilities without touching the core
  • Prepare for S/4HANA on their own timeline

6. A Phased, Utility‑Friendly Adoption Model

Phase 1: Observe

  • Identify custom code hotspots
  • Monitor usage, performance, and change frequency

Phase 2: Isolate

  • Move non‑core logic to BTP extensions
  • Replace enhancements with APIs and events

Phase 3: Optimize

  • Retire unused Z‑objects
  • Standardize extension patterns
  • Accelerate future innovation

This approach delivers value within existing budgets and without destabilizing operations. 

7. Final Thought for Utility Leaders

The goal is not to eliminate custom code overnight. The goal is to stop letting yesterday’s decisions dictate tomorrow’s options.

SAP BTP provides a practical, low‑risk path to reduce technical debt, lower long‑term cost, and modernize SAP IS‑U — without a big bang and without compromising the core.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture, not migration dates.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture — not migration dates.

entry.title: Media Handling in SAP CAPM – Backend and Frontend Implementation
entry.content:
Hi,

Introduction:

Media handling is a key requirement in enterprise applications for uploading, storing, and retrieving files like PDFs and documents. The Cloud Application Programming Model (CAPM) from SAP enables efficient media management through OData Service services. In this blog, we implement end to end media handling with backend and UI5 integration

Backend Implementation 

  • Create database schema  
  • Create service definition 
  • Implement custom logic 

Step 1: 

First, we define an entity to store file metadata and binary content 

For that create one cds file under db folder 

namespace mediahanling;

entity myMedia{
    key ID: UUID;
    filename: String;
    mediatype: String;
    content: LargeBinary @Core.MediaType : mediatype;
}

This entity stores uploaded files and their metadata 

Mediatype: MIME type of file (ex: image/png, application/pdf) 

Content (LargeBinary): Stores actual file data in binary format 

Content: LargeBinary @Core.MediaType: mediatype:  

  • Content is a media stream 
  • Media type comes from mediatype field 
  • Enables file upload/download through OData 

Step2: 

  • Now we have to expose the entity as a OData service 
  • So, create cds file under srv folder 
using { mediahanling } from '../db/schema';

service MyMediaService {
    entity MediaEntity as projection on mediahanling.myMedia;
}

Step 3: 

  • Now we will create js file under srv folder and we will implement business logic to validate uploads 
const cds= require("@sap/cds");

module.exports = cds.service.impl(function(){
    const { MediaEntity } = this.entities;
    this.before('CREATE', MediaEntity , async(req)=>{
        if(!req.data.filename){
            req.error(400, "File name required");
        }
        if(!req.data.mediatype){
            req.error(400, "Media type required");
        }
        console.log("Uploading:", req.data.filename);
    })
})

Business logic is written inside service handler. Service.js file adds custom validation logic before file is stored in the database 

Const cds = require(‘@sap/cds’)  

  • Imports CAP runtime from SAP SE 
  • Provides APIs to define services, handlers and events 
  • Required to implement custom logic in CAP Application 

Module.exports = cds.service .impl(function(){}) 

  • Registers custom logic for your service 
  • CAP automatically call this when the service starts

Const {MediaEntity} = this.entities

  • Gets the exposed in service.cds 
  • MediaEntity represents your media uppload table 
  • We will this reference to attach event handler 

As of now we have Defined Entity and exposed entity as a OData Service 

Now we will upload the PDF file, for that we need to create http file

And I have created PDF folder and under PDF folder I have PDF file

 

test.http file

###
POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/my-media/MediaEntity
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "filename": "sample.pdf",
  "mediatype": "application/pdf"
}


###
PUT http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/my-media/MediaEntity(74382f14-ff18-405e-9ce9-fcdf84317f02)/content
Content-Type: application/pdf

< ./pdf/sample.pdf

When you click on send request, this request creates new media record in our CAP Service 

It stores file details (metadata) but it will not upload the actual file content

 

  • When you click on send request it, it will create metadata, it will look like this 
  • Since we have given UUID for ID in entity, Id will create automatically

Now have to take that ID, and keep it in PUT request 

  • Then, click on send request, then it is going to upload pdf 
  • Click on MediaEntity and we can see the data 

As of now we have done backend implementation, now we will do frontend implementation 

  • For that we need to create UI5 application 

 

  • While creating UI5 app, select Data Source as a Local CAP project 
  • Then choose, our CAP project and then select OData service

Once you created UI5 app, we can see UI5 app is created under App folder 

In view I have used required controls to upload file and display PDF 

<mvc:View controllerName="mediahandling.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:unified="sap.ui.unified">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <unified:FileUploader id="fileUploader" width="400px" height="300px" change="onFileChange" uploadOnChange="false" >
        </unified:FileUploader>
        <Button text="Submit" press="onSumbit" ></Button>

        <ScrollContainer horizontal="true" vertical="true">
                <FlexBox class="sapUiSmallMargin" direction="Column" renderType="Div" >
                    <FlexBox direction="Row" fitContainer="true" renderType="Div">
                        <PDFViewer class="sapUiSmallMarginEnd" source="{/Source}" title="PDF" height="70vh" width="100vw" isTrustedSource="true">
                            <layoutData>
                                <FlexItemData growFactor="1"/>
                            </layoutData>
                        </PDFViewer>
			        </FlexBox>
                </FlexBox>
        </ScrollContainer>

        
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

In controller.js file I have written some logic to upload file and display the PDF in UI 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], (Controller,MessageToast) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("mediahandling.controller.View1", {
        
        onInit() {
            debugger;
            this.getView().setModel(new sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel({
                Source: ""
            }));

                
        },
         onFileChange: function (oEvent) {
                 debugger;
                 this._file = oEvent.getParameter("files")[0];
        },
        onSumbit:function(oEvent){
            debugger;
              var oFile = this._file;

            if (!oFile) {
                MessageToast.show("Select file first");
                return;
            }

            if (oFile.type !== "application/pdf") {
                MessageToast.show("Upload PDF only");
                return;
            }

            var that = this;
            fetch("/odata/v4/my-media/MediaEntity", {
                method: "POST",
                headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                body: JSON.stringify({
                    filename: oFile.name,
                    mediatype: oFile.type
                })
            })

            .then(function (res) {

                if (!res.ok) {
                    throw new Error("Metadata creation failed");
                }

                return res.json();
            })

            .then(function (data) {

                that.mediaID = data.ID;

                return fetch("/odata/v4/my-media/MediaEntity(" + that.mediaID + ")/content", {
                    method: "PUT",
                    headers: { "Content-Type": oFile.type },
                    body: oFile
                });
            })

            .then(function (res) {

                if (!res.ok) {
                    throw new Error("File upload failed");
                }

                MessageToast.show("Upload successful");

                var sUrl = "/odata/v4/my-media/MediaEntity(" + that.mediaID + ")/content";

                that.getView().getModel().setProperty("/Source", sUrl);
            })

            .catch(function (err) {
                console.error(err);
                MessageToast.show("Upload failed — check console");
            });




        }
    });
});

Putput:

Conclusion: 

In this blog, we have implemented complete media handling by creating a CAP entity, exposing it as an OData service, and uploading files from backend and frontend. We also built a UI5 application to upload and display PDF files, providing a scalable solution for managing media in enterprise applications

entry.title: Part 2: Creating and Deploying a CAP Application with PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the Second Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone.

 

Introduction

In the Part 1 of this series, a CAP application has been created and deployed to SAP BTP using SAP HANA Cloud. In the current blog, I will focus on creating and deploying a CAP application to BTP using the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option provided by SAP. This approach demonstrates a PostgreSQL-first workflow and focuses on the specific configuration required to use PostgreSQL as the persistence layer.

 

Why PostgreSQL on SAP BTP

You might have a question, “If a CAP application can be deployed with SAP HANA Cloud, then why go with an alternative Database?”

While the SAP HANA Cloud is still the best choice for enterprise applications, CAP also allows seamless integration with alternative databases with minimal changes.

PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP is useful in scenarios such as:

  • Cost-sensitive environments
  • Open-source aligned architectures
  • Hybrid cloud landscapes

From the CAP application perspective, the CDS model and service definitions remain unchanged.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option service subcription
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a New CAP Project in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-postgres-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: PostgreSQL
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

CAP project creation wizard

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Define the Data Model

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;

entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

 

Expose the Model via a CAP Service

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

Add Data

Run the following in BAS Terminal

cds add data –records 5

 

Run the Application locally

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build

cds deploy

 

Add a Managed Approuter

  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

This is important to view the data once deployed.

Prepare the Project for Deployment

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

Though everything is ready, you need to make a small change for Postgres Deployment. Make the following change:

  • In MTA, go to resources
  • Navigate to <app-name>-postgres
  • Change the service-plan from development to trial

 

mta.yaml visible in the BAS workspace

 

Note : Do this only if you are using a Trial Account. If you are in an enterprise account, please go to your Instances and Subscription and copy the service-plan from PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option and look for the plan under Service Plan.

 

Configure CAP to Use PostgreSQL

Navigate into the project folder and install the PostgreSQL driver:

cds add postgres

Note: This step is not important as BAS Template must have already added this. This could be used while creating the CAP application without the Template wizard.

 

Build the CAP Project

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-postgres-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry Deployment

 

During deployment

  • PostgreSQL credentials are injected automatically via service binding
  • CAP initializes the database schema
  • The application starts without additional configuration

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

Deploying an SAP CAP application with the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP highlights CAP’s database-agnostic architecture. The CDS models and service logic remain unchanged, while PostgreSQL is integrated seamlessly through minimal configuration and service binding.

This approach provides flexibility for cost-sensitive and open-source aligned environments while still leveraging SAP BTP’s enterprise capabilities such as automated schema deployment, secure credential management, and simplified lifecycle handling through Cloud Foundry and MTA deployment.

Overall, PostgreSQL on SAP BTP enables developers to build scalable, cloud-native CAP applications with greater persistence flexibility, while retaining the option to transition to SAP HANA Cloud in the future without significant code changes.

 

Next Steps

In the next part of this series, the deployed CAP application will be integrated with SAP Build Work Zone, demonstrating how backend services can be exposed through a secure, user-friendly interface to deliver a complete end-to-end enterprise application experience.

entry.title: Part 1: How to create CAPM project in BAS
entry.content:
Introduction: You could build a complete enterprise application - database, APIs, and UI - without different technologies? Well, answer is CAPM (Cloud Application Programming Model). CAPM  is a framework that provides a set of languages, libraries, and tools that allow you to build a complete full-stack application - covering the database, service layer, and UI.
It gives you everything you need in one coherent package: database modeling, service creation, and UI generation. No more context switching between completely different tools.

What we're building today:
  • A complete Employee-Department management system
  • From data model to working UI 

In this blog, we will create a simple full-stack CAP application and learn how to make basic connections between the data model, service, and UI.

We will cover the following topics:

  • Project creation

  • Data model creation

  • Service creation

  • UI creation

Step 1: Create a CAPM Project in BAS

To build a CAP project in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), you must use a Full Stack Cloud Application dev space.

Once your dev space is created and running, open it and proceed to Step 2.

Step 2: Open Terminal

Inside your BAS workspace:

  • Go to Terminal -> New Terminal
  • Run the following command to initialize a CAP project:

cds init demo1

This generates the project with the following structure:

  • db/ – Defines your entities (data model)

  • srv/ – Exposes your services

  • app/ – Your UI application.

Step 3: Create the Data Model

Create a file under db/ named schema.cds and add the following code:

namespace demo1;
using { cuid  } from '@sap/cds/common';


entity  Employees : cuid {
     name : String(50);
     adress : String(100);
     emailId: String(100);
     city: String(50);
     country : String(50);
     designation: String(50);
     salary: Decimal(10, 2);
     department: Association to Departments;
}

entity Departments : cuid {
     name : String(50);
     location : String(100);
     budget : Decimal(15, 2);
     employee: Association to many Employees
}

Step 4: Create the Service

Create a service.cds file under srv/ and expose your entities as a service:

 

using { demo1 as dataModel} from '../db/schema';

service EmployeeDepartmentService{
    entity Employees as projection on dataModel.Employees;
    entity Departments as projection on dataModel.Departments;
}

Now run the command in terminal

cds watch

Note: If you see "connection was rejected" Run the following:

npm uninstall better-sqlite3
npm install better-sqlite3

Then execute cds watch again.

Step 5: Test Using service.http

Create a file named service.http and add the following:

# GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/employee-department/Employees

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/employee-department/Employees
Content-Type: application/json

{
    "name": "Nila", 
    "adress": "Chennai", 
    "emailId": "nila@gmail.com", 
    "city": "Anytown", 
    "country": "USA", 
    "designation": "Software Engineer", 
    "salary": "40000.00"
}

Click Send Request and you should receive 200 OK if everything works correctly.

Step 6: Create the UI5 Application

  1. Go to File → Create Project from Template
  2. Select List Report Object Page (or Freestyle UI5 — here we choose List Report)
  3. Click Next

Data Source & Service Selection

  • Data Source: Use a local CAP project

  • Choose Project: Select demo1

  • OData Service: It will automatically pick EmployeeDepartmentService

Click Next

Entity Selection

  • Main Entity: Employees
  • Table Type: Grid
    Click Next

Project Name

  • Enter: demo1ui
  • Click Finish

Your UI5 application is now generated.

step 7: View the UI

  • You can run the UI by opening index.html inside the web application below.
  • You can also check the metadata and OData results directly.

Step 8: Use “Show Page Map”

  • Right-click webapp → Show Page Map.

This provides a visual overview of the app structure.
From here, you can create:

  • Table columns
  • Sections
  • Actions
  • Object Page content, etc..

Below I added the required table columns.

Step 9: Object Page Navigation

Once the user clicks an entry from the List Report, the app navigates to the Object Page and displays the configured fields.

Step 10: Test Data in UI

This is the initial screen After sending the POST request from service.http, refresh your UI5 app.

 The inserted records will appear in the table by clicking Go.

Note: SQLite is used in-memory for local CAP testing.
This means data is cleared every time the server stops, this is for development mode.

Conclusion:  This blog is just the starting point for learning CAPM. Understanding these basic concepts is very important. You now understand how CAP connects database, services, and UI seamlessly. This foundation will help you build real business application

entry.title: SAP CAP on HANA XSA: Scaling Industrial Data Applications Beyond the Cloud
entry.content:
In large-scale industrial data processing, handling high volumes of data is not an "edge case"—it is the standard. When thousands or millions of data points from sensors and machinery must be processed in real-time and synchronized via automated jobs, your architecture determines whether the system thrives or grinds to a halt. 

In this article, I will show you how we implemented a Mission-Critical Industrial Data Application using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) on SAP HANA 2.0 XSA (On-Premise). This combination is rarely discussed, as the CAP framework is primarily known for BTP-based development, but on-premise, it reveals immense power when paired correctly with HANA.

Why Customers Still Rely on On-Premise XSA

While the "Cloud First" strategy is dominant, many customers—especially in industries with critical infrastructure—continue to use SAP HANA XSA On-Premise. Key reasons include:

  • Data Sovereignty & Privacy: Some organizations handle highly sensitive industrial or national data that must remain behind the company firewall for security reasons.

  • Regulatory Compliance: Strict legal requirements in certain sectors prohibit the use of public cloud environments for specific types of operational data.

  • Minimal Latency: In industrial environments, being physically close to the data source (the machines and sensors) minimizes network latency, which is crucial for high-speed processing.

  • Legacy Integration: Direct proximity to existing On-Premise HANA 2.0 landscapes often makes a local XSA deployment more efficient than a hybrid cloud setup.

1. Visualizing the Architecture

To bring it all together, here is the architectural overview of the Industrial Data Application:

2. The XSA Setup: Exporting CAP as a Module

Unlike in SAP BTP, the XSA environment requires an explicit configuration so the controller can correctly bind the service and manage port assignments. 

The Entry Point: srv/server.js

In an XSA infrastructure, the Node.js module must explicitly export its server handle. While CAP automates much of this in the cloud, on-premise XSA requires this manual step in the server.js:

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

// Optional: Bootstrap for custom middlewares (e.g., OData V2 Proxy)
cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => {
    /* Custom logic, e.g., security headers or logging */
});

// CRITICAL for XSA: Export the server handle.
// This allows the XSA runtime to bind the service to the platform.
module.exports = cds.server;

In the package.json, we reference this via "start": "node srv/server.js".

The Deployment Blueprint: mta.yaml

The mta.yaml is the heart of the infrastructure. To handle parallel loads and optimize deployment, we use specific parameters:

_schema-version: "3.1.0"
ID: industrial_app_platform
version: 1.0.0
description: "Industrial Data Platform - High Performance CAP on XSA"

parameters:
  enable-parallel-deployments: true

modules:
  # --- SRV Module (CAP) ---
  - name: industrial_app-srv
    type: nodejs
    path: .
    parameters:
      buildpack: sap_nodejs_buildpack
      memory: 512M
      health-check-type: http
      health-check-http-endpoint: /health
      env:
        HDB_NODEJS_THREADPOOL_SIZE: "8" # Optimized for parallel DB throughput
    build-parameters:
      builder: custom
      commands:
        - npm ci
        - npx cds build --production
      build-result: gen/srv
    provides:
      - name: srv_api
        properties:
          url: '${default-url}'

    requires:
      - name: industrial_app-uaa
      - name: industrial_app-hdi

  # --- DB Module (HANA) ---
  - name: industrial_app-db
    type: hdb
    path: gen/db
    parameters:
      memory: 512M
    build-parameters:
      build-result: .
      ignore: ["default-*.json", "*node_modules*"]
    requires:
      - name: industrial_app-hdi

  # --- App Router ---
  - name: industrial_app-approuter
    type: approuter.nodejs
    path: approuter
    requires:
      - name: industrial_app-uaa
      - name: srv_api
        group: destinations
        properties:
          name: srv_api
          url: ~{url}
          forwardAuthToken: true
    parameters:
      memory: 256M
      disk-quota: 512M

resources:
  - name: industrial_app-uaa
    type: com.sap.xs.uaa
    parameters:
      config_path: ./xs-security.json

  - name: industrial_app-hdi
    type: com.sap.xs.hdi-container
    parameters:
      schema: INDUSTRIAL_APP_SCHEMA
      makeUniqueName: true

3. Evolution: From "Pure Node.js" to SAP CAP

Previously, such industrial solutions were often developed as pure Node.js/Express applications. However, moving to CAP offers massive advantages:

  • Efficiency: CAP provides the OData infrastructure and security (via @(requires: 'Viewer')) automatically.

  • Focus: We concentrate on the Domain Model (CDS), while the framework handles the "plumbing."

  • Hybrid Approach: Local development on SQLite, productive power on HANA.

4. The Database Layer: Native Performance with HDI

When millions of records are being ingested, JavaScript object mapping can become a bottleneck. Here, we "drop down" to the database level using specific artifacts in the db folder.

Configuration: .hdiconfig

For HANA to know how to handle our native files, we need an .hdiconfig. It assigns the correct plugins to the file extensions:

{
  "file_extensions": {
    "hdbprocedure": { "plugin_name": "sap.hana.di.procedure" },
    "cds": { "plugin_name": "sap.hana.di.cds" }
  }
}

Native Power: .hdbprocedure

By using a Stored Procedure, we leverage Table-Valued Parameters (TVPs) to pass data packets directly to the HANA core, bypassing framework overhead:

-- Anonymized Procedure for Mass Ingestion
PROCEDURE "proc_handle_mass_ingestion" (
  IN p_timestamps TABLE ( series_ID NVARCHAR(36), seq INTEGER, ts TIMESTAMP ),
  IN p_values TABLE ( series_ID NVARCHAR(36), seq INTEGER, val NVARCHAR(20) )
)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT SQL SECURITY INVOKER AS
BEGIN
  -- High-efficiency batch insert using SYSUUID
  INSERT INTO "DATA_STORE_TIMESTAMPS" ("ID", "SERIES_ID", "SEQ", "TS")
  SELECT SYSUUID, series_ID, seq, ts FROM :p_timestamps;

  INSERT INTO "DATA_STORE_VALUES" ("ID", "SERIES_ID", "SEQ", "VAL")
  SELECT SYSUUID, series_ID, seq, val FROM :p_values;
END;

The Handover: Calling the Procedure from Node.js

This is where the performance magic happens. Instead of calling INSERT thousands of times, we pass the entire array of data to the procedure in a single tx.run call:

// Inside your CAP Service Logic (srv/handlers.js)
async function bulkInsertData(tx, seriesResults) {
    // 1. Prepare the input arrays (matching the TVP structure)
    const timestampsForHANA = [];
    const valuesForHANA = [];

    seriesResults.forEach(res => {
        res.timestamps.forEach(t => timestampsForHANA.push([res.sdId, t.seq, t.timestamp]));
        res.values.forEach(v => valuesForHANA.push([res.sdId, v.seq, v.value]));
    });

    // 2. Execute the procedure via CAP's transaction object
    // Passing the arrays as parameters (TVPs)
    await tx.run('CALL "proc_handle_mass_ingestion"(?, ?)', [
        timestampsForHANA, 
        valuesForHANA
    ]);
}

5. Automation & Real-Time: SAP Job Scheduler

To keep data current, we do not use simple internal app intervals. Instead, we use the SAP Job Scheduler. It triggers our import logic reliably from the outside at defined periodic intervals.

Once the job is completed and the mass data has been processed via the Stored Procedure, real-time updates are triggered in the Angular frontend. By using CAP events or targeted OData refresh triggers in the UI, we ensure that users always see the latest status of industrial assets without needing to reload the page.

6. Conclusion: The Best of Both Worlds

By combining SAP CAP's productivity with the raw performance of HANA Stored Procedures and the SAP Job Scheduler, the IDP platform manages massive data volumes with ease. We use CAP for 90% of the application and native HANA features where performance is the absolute priority.

How are you handling mass data imports in CAP? Ever experienced and/or applied SAP CAP on HANA XSA? I look forward to your comments!

Cheers

Rufat

entry.title: Major New Version of the hana-cli: How It Was Built with the Help of AI (and a Lot of Human Review)
entry.content:
This week I released the newest hana-cli version, and I wanted to share what actually happened behind the scenes. Not just the feature list, but the real process since this was the first version with significant AI assistance. The small steps, the rough edges, and the lessons learned.

TL;DR Code generation helped the project add more features in two months than I'd been able to deliver in the last two years; but quality still came from discipline, architecture, and a ton of testing.

hana-cli HanaCloud command that lists details of HANA Cloud Instances for the target BTP SubAccount

What Changed in the 4.0 Release

4.0 release was not a small maintenance version. It was a structural and architectural shift for the tool.

In this release, I pushed major updates across runtime, architecture, test coverage, docs, and developer workflow. Looking at the git history since January 2026, the repo saw 235 commits so far, with a huge concentration in February and March. This was not a patch release cycle. This was the kind of work that changes how a tool feels to use (hopefully for the better).

The version line moved to 4.x, which tells you something important: substantial internal changes happened. We migrated to Express 5, now that CAP supports it. The MCP server evolved from an experimental rough draft into something that actually feels almost production-ready, with stronger tool registration patterns, better resource handling, and practical guidance for setup and troubleshooting.

Under the hood, database connection handling had to be redesigned to support MCP and multi-environment execution better. CLI startup performance improved significantly through lazy loading and faster execution paths. This is an item that's bothered me personally for a while and that I've tried to tackle in the past. We made import and export commands much stronger, adding richer import options, better schema handling, and more thoughtful validation behavior but still with a strict focus on keeping the processing client side. Internationalization was finally a focus (thanks to machine translation), with full translation bundles for additional locales like German, Spanish, French, Japanese, Korean, Portuguese, Simplified Chinese, Hindi, and Polish.

One of the bigger quality-of-life improvements was consistency work across our very large command surface. This is where AI coding assistants really shine. They helped normalize parameter naming, improved how the tool suggests fixes for unknown commands and options, and made sure 200+ commands follow the same conventions. The docs got rebuilt from scratch with a modern VitePress site and better technical guides. And the tests scaled hard. We went from roughly 40 percent coverage on critical paths to roughly 85 percent. That matters more than people think. And this was actually the first thing I did before I would start using AI. Without more tests I wouldn't have trusted AI to make some of the consistency changes. In the end, the unit and E2E tests will really help when introducing new features in the future, because after each small change you can run the entire battery of tests. If it's just me testing by hand I often can't test every edge case or every command variation. With a strong test suite, I can be more confident that the changes I make do not break existing behavior.

The Architecture Shift That Matters Most

For me, the most important architecture story in 4.x is this: we moved from "a big bag of commands" toward "a more intentional platform". Instead of four separate operating modes living in isolation, CLI mode, interactive mode, API server mode, and MCP mode now feel like parts of one coherent system. That might sound abstract, but it changed how I approach new features.

The MCP server is a good example. It went from experimental command passthrough to something that actually feels like a real integration layer. Instead of duplicating command definition, it dynamically reads from the CLI setup or from the help output. We built better registration patterns, better metadata handling, and practical troubleshooting guidance. When I want to add a new command now, I do not have to think about five different places to integrate it. The pattern is clear: dynamic discovery and reuse was the only way to continue to scale.

Connection and execution flows became more deliberate too. We handle profile-based and CDS-based cases much more consistently now, with stronger error messaging and fewer of those fragile edge cases that only show up in production. Performance was treated as a product feature, not an afterthought. Startup path optimization and lazy loading were not "nice to have". They changed day-to-day usability in a real way for common, frequent used commands.

hana-cli UI mode with SAPUI5 based interface

How I Used GitHub Copilot in Small, Incremental Steps

I did not sit down and ask for one giant AI-generated rewrite. That sounded like a recipe for disaster to me.

Instead, I worked in small, intentional slices. Start with one behavior. Write a few targeted tests. Implement a narrow change. Re-run all the tests. Refine. Repeat. This rhythm sounds obvious until you are staring at the opportunity to ask Copilot to "just generate the whole feature", which is precisely when discipline matters most.

A key part of the workflow was starting with hand-coded examples first. I wrote the initial 7 to 8 unit tests manually to establish the baseline style and what really mattered. Those tests acted like calibration points. They showed the agent what I thought good tests look like in this codebase. After that, I used the coding agent to extrapolate safely. Generate related scenarios. Extend edge-case coverage. Replicate patterns across similar commands. Fill out the repetitive but necessary test variations. That gave me speed without surrendering control.

The other way that I used AI was for parallel work. This project has long been a weekend/late night/passion project for me. This isn't part of my day job. But free time is precious and limited.
