I've had a long list of features and wishes for this project that just never seemed to get shorter. With AI coding agents I could kick off tasks while I'm working during the day. I could go to a meeting for TechEd 2026 and in my other window, an AI agent would be running tests or renaming parameters. At the end of the day I could review the results, make adjustments, and keep the momentum going. It turned AI from a "weekend experiment" into a "day-to-day teammate". Although a teammate that sometimes felt like it had amnesia. Occasionally it would repeat a mistake it made a few days prior. Or it would completely forget a critical architectural pattern that I had painstakingly documented in the repo instructions. That is where the next section comes in.

Why the .github Folder Was Critical

If I had to point to one thing that made AI-assisted work sustainable in this repo, it is the .github customization layer. This is where the project-specific memory and operating model live. No longer did have a team of minions with short term memory loss, but a team of agents with a shared playbook and role-specific instructions. I finally started seeing real consistency across multiple requests.

Project Structure and the .github folder with content that influences GitHub CoPilot

Here is how each layer of that system actually works.

copilot-instructions.md — the always-on baseline

This file is loaded into every single agent session when this project is the target, regardless of what you ask or which files are open. It is the unconditional context layer. In this project it does a few things: it redirects the agent to the project-overview.instructions.md file for deeper architecture context, it states the non-negotiables (Node.js ESM module format, i18n text bundles for every user-facing string, VitePress docs structure), and it sets the behavioral guardrails (minimal changes, no new dependencies without human approval, do not touch docs or workflows unless explicitly asked). Think of it as the onboarding document that every new teammate has to read before their first commit. The agent reads it before every conversation.

.github/instructions/*.instructions.md — file-scope rules with automatic injection

This repo has 25 instruction files, each targeted at a specific area: cli-command-development.instructions.md, testing.instructions.md, route-development.instructions.md, i18n-translation-management.instructions.md, mcp-server-development.instructions.md, vitepress-config-management.instructions.md, and so on. Each file has an applyTo front-matter field that is a glob pattern, for example bin/*.js or tests/**/*.Test.js or docs/.vitepress/**/*.{ts,js,json}. When the agent touches a file that matches a glob, the corresponding instruction file is automatically pulled into context without you having to ask. So when the agent is editing a command file in bin/, it automatically picks up the rules about yargs structure, required JSDoc, i18n bundle usage, error handling shape, and database connection patterns. When it is editing a test file, it automatically picks up the Mocha conventions, assertion style, and the utilities it should reuse. This is the mechanism that prevented AI "drift". The rules followed the files, not the conversation.

.github/agents/*.agent.md — specialized agent personas

Each agent file defines a named role that can be invoked explicitly. The YAML declares a name, a description, and a list of allowed tools. The body defines the agent's job, its constraints, its approach steps, and its expected output format. The CLI Agent (cli.agent.md) is scoped to bin/, routes/, utils/, and related tests and is explicitly constrained from touching documentation unless asked. The DocOps Agent (docops.agent.md) knows the docs taxonomy and sidebar structure. The MCP Agent (mcp.agent.md) enforces JSON-RPC compliance and tool registration patterns. The Tooling Agent (tooling.agent.md) handles automation scripts, npm scripts, and GitHub workflows with an eye on cross-platform compatibility. The Version Maintenance Agent orchestrates the full release choreography: version bumps across multiple package.json files, SAPUI5 version updates, changelog management, and documentation synchronization. The E2E Test Agent specializes in multi-step CLI testing scenarios. What this means in practice is that the agent does not have to figure out what role to play from scratch each time. It is handed a specific operating mode with explicit scope and constraints baked in.

.github/skills/*/SKILL.md — bundled, reusable workflows

Skills are a level above agents. Where an agent defines a persona, a skill defines a procedure. Each skill folder contains a SKILL.md that lists the steps to follow for a complete workflow, the instruction files that apply, and the references the agent should load. This repo has three skills: cli-command-development, docs-automation, and mcp-server-workflows. The cli-command-development skill, for example, tells the agent to load the CLI command instructions, the route instructions if a route file is involved, the i18n instructions, the testing instructions, and the test-utilities-reuse instructions, then work through them in sequence. Skills eliminate the overhead of re-explaining which instruction files are relevant and in what order to apply them. They encode accumulated workflow knowledge so the agent does not have to reconstruct it on the fly.

.github/prompts/*.prompt.md — ready-made entry points for common tasks

Prompt files are parameterized task templates. Each one has a name, a description, an argument-hint that tells the user what input to provide, and a body that pre-fills the task framing the agent would otherwise have to reconstruct. For example, update-markdown-docs.prompt.md instructs the agent to read the existing doc style, follow the command-documentation rules when working in docs/02-commands/, keep changes minimal, and then report both the edits and a consistency cross-check. Other prompts cover updating GitHub workflows, VitePress config, MCP server code, tsconfig files, and package.json scripts. Using a prompt file instead of a freeform question means the agent starts from a well-defined task structure rather than having to interpret a vague request and risk misunderstanding the scope.

.github/hooks/ and quality-gates.json — deterministic guardrails

This is the most technical layer. The hooks are three Node.js scripts — pre-tool-use.js, post-tool-use.js, and stop.js — wired up via quality-gates.json as lifecycle interceptors. The quality-gates.json file maps hook names to commands: PreToolUse runs pre-tool-use.js before every tool call, PostToolUse runs post-tool-use.js after every tool call that modifies files, and Stop runs stop.js when the agent finishes a session. The pre-hook inspects the tool call payload and blocks a list of risky commands outright: npm install, npm publish, git push, git reset --hard, rm -rf, and similar destructive or irreversible operations.

If the agent tries to run any of those, the hook intercepts it and returns a blocking response before the command ever executes. The post-hook checks what files changed in the working tree after each tool use, runs targeted lint checks if source files changed, and surfaces any new errors to the agent so it can self-correct before moving to the next step. The stop hook runs a final summary of what changed in the session. This made a real difference: instead of discovering that the agent had introduced a linting error three steps after the fact, the post-hook caught it immediately and the agent fixed it in place. It turned error correction from a cleanup pass at the end into a continuous loop within the session.

This structure sounds elaborate until you use it. What it does is turn Copilot from "autocomplete with opinions" into "a teammate with repo-specific understanding".

The agent stayed aligned with project patterns, especially around ESM syntax, i18n text handling, and command conventions. I could steer style and scope per task type instead of hoping the AI would guess correctly. Generated code drifted away less from project norms. I got safer behavior around risky commands through hooks and guardrails. After you read a few horror stories online about AI wiping out a repo or an entire drive, you see why this is so important. And we preserved architectural context across long sessions and many edits so the tool never forgot why things were designed a certain way.

Each agent came with matching instruction files that spelled out the specific rules, patterns, and anti-patterns for that domain. When I needed to add a new command, I asked the CLI Agent, and it immediately understood command structure, i18n requirements, test patterns, and where to hook into the command metadata. When I wanted to regenerate all command docs at once, I asked the DocOps Agent, and it knew the taxonomy categories, sidebar structure, and the exact generation scripts to use.

hana-cli's New Documentation Site

Fast Code Generation Is Real, but So Is Review Work

Code generation is fast. Review is not optional.

Even with strong prompts and repo instructions, I still spent many full days validating behavior changes across a 200-plus command surface. Checking cross-command consistency, particularly parameter naming, schema alignment, and error handling patterns. Running tests and fixing failures, especially the tricky cross-platform compatibility issues between Windows, Linux, and macOS. Reviewing docs and examples for completeness and accuracy. Manually verifying edge cases that looked correct but were not robust enough. Testing UI components and WebSocket behavior in the browser. Validating MCP tool registrations and JSON-RPC message flow.

This is the part some people skip when they tell the AI productivity story as pure Vibe coding. The output quality that we hopefully have in hana-cli came from that review loop, not from generation alone.

The Testing Story

One of the biggest wins this cycle was test coverage. We went from something like 40 percent coverage on critical paths to around 85 percent. Did code generation write all those tests? No. But it made it fast enough that writing comprehensive tests became practical instead of aspirational.

The pattern that worked was to hand-write 7 to 8 seed tests that show the ideal test structure and what you actually care about validating. Then ask the agent to extend those, creating related scenarios, error cases, and variations. Verify every generated test by running it locally and checking it actually catches bugs. Keep the tests you trust, delete the rest. By the end, we had better coverage and faster iteration. Both mattered.

What Took the Most Time

If you ask me where the real work happened, it was not generation. Creating the code went from taking most of the time to probably only about 5% of the time. Architecture decisions about how commands should register with the MCP server. Parameter passing design. Connection flow refactoring. It was writing the tests first, before or alongside the feature. It was validating that the generated code actually works by running it, breaking it on purpose, fixing it. It was keeping docs in sync every time we changed command behavior. It was cross-platform testing to ensure Windows and Linux do not have surprises. And it was reviewing for consistency, making sure 200-plus commands all follow the same conventions.

That is all human work. Generation helped with the repetitive parts. Discipline came from the human part of the project.

My Honest Take After This Release

Copilot did not replace engineering here. It amplified it.

The wins came from combining clear architecture that you can reference and extend. Repo-specific agent customization so the tool understands your project, not just general code. Small incremental development instead of big risky changes. Human-authored seed tests so the AI has good examples to learn from. And relentless validation through testing and review of every piece.

If you are building serious tooling, that balance works. Skip the validation half and you are gambling.

For this release, I am proud of both the speed and the discipline. And yes, I am also proud that we made it feel better for users while modernizing a lot under the hood.

Special thanks to everyone who uses hana-cli and reports issues. That feedback drives where we invest next.

entry.title: Real-Time Architecture in SAP CAP: Mastering Asynchronous Jobs, Gateways with WebSockets
entry.content:
In my previous article (SAP CAP on HANA XSA), we explored how the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) on SAP HANA 2.0 XSA provides the raw power needed for high-performance industrial applications, specifically focusing on mass data ingestion via native Stored Procedures.

However, processing high volumes of data in real-tme is only half the battle. In a mission-critical Industrial Data Application, the user interface must stay in sync with these background processes. If a mass import takes several minutes, a standard synchronous request will lead to Gateway Timeouts (HTTP 502) and a frustrated user.

This second part of our series focuses on how to close this gap by combining CAP, the SAP Job Scheduler, and Express WebSockets into a responsive real-time architecture.

While the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is most commonly associated with the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), it offers immense productivity on-premise as well. However, the challenge lies in harmonizing long-running backend jobs (e.g., mass data imports via Stored Procedures) with the need for real-time updates in the frontend (Angular UI) under XSA security and routing constraints (Approuter). Relying on purely synchronous requests in this context risks gateway timeouts and a poor user experience.

In this post, I present a responsive real-time architecture that closes these gaps by combining CAP, SAP Job Scheduler, Angular WebSockets, and native HANA Stored Procedures.

Architecture at a Glance

Our high-level architecture diagram divides the Industrial Data Application into logical layers. The central data flow is categorized into color-coded zones representing the Client, Gateway, Service, Data, and External Systems.

Core Components of the Architecture:

  • Client Layer (Purple): An Angular UI providing real-time charting dashboards. It communicates with the backend via HTTPS OData APIs and WSS (WebSockets) Event subscriptions.

  • Gateway Layer (Green): The SAP Approuter serves as the central entry point and gateway. It manages API destinations, handles JWT tokens for authentication (linked to the XSUAA/UAA provider), and routes both HTTPS (OData/Actions) and WebSocket traffic.

  • Service Layer (Blue): An SAP CAP Node.js backend extended with express-ws. It contains the standard OData CRUD layer, business logic, and specialized mass data handlers. Central to this is the Express WebSocket Endpoint with a client registry to manage push notifications. This layer is also integrated with the SAP Job Scheduler.

  • Data Layer (Orange): The SAP HANA 2.0 XSA on-premise platform. An HDI Container stores the primary application data in the Column Store. Native HANA Stored Procedures (SQLScript) are used to execute the actual mass data calls and imports.

  • External Systems (Amber): External industrial APIs, historians, or sensor interfaces that act as the source for massive data streams. Native HANA procedures communicate with these systems for data ingestion.

Technical Implementation: WebSocket Registry and Broadcasting

To transmit real-time events efficiently to the Angular UI, we use express-ws within the CAP Node.js backend. This requires a structured registry to manage WebSocket clients based on their specific interests (event types or channels).

In our project, we defined the WebSocket channels as follows. This registry utilizes Sets to store unique WebSocket connections for each business event type:

// db/src/ws/wsRegistry.js (Example location)
'use strict';

// Business-specific registration of WebSocket channels
const wsClients = {
    'validation-status': new Set(), // Critical for the numbered workflow
    'import-status': new Set(),
    'committing-status': new Set(),
    'industrial-trade': new Set(),
    'systemStatusChanged': new Set(),
    'UserChanged': new Set(),
    'process_step': new Set(),
    'job_scheduler': new Set(),
    'program-input-mode': new Set(),
    'batch-job-status': new Set(),
};

module.exports = { wsClients };

The Asynchronous Job Workflow

The most critical part of this architecture is the asynchronous job flow, numbered 1–6 in the diagram. This flow is a best practice for industrial scenarios where jobs cannot be completed synchronously. It demonstrates how CAP and WebSockets prevent gateway timeouts.

Let’s break down the steps:

  1. Angular UI triggers CAP Action (Client -> Service): The process starts when the Angular UI triggers a CAP Action (or service call) via HTTPS. This follows the standard HTTP OData/Actions path.

  2. Fast HTTP 202 Response (Service -> Client): This is a decisive step for UX and timeout prevention. Instead of waiting for the job to finish, the CAP service responds immediately with an HTTP 202 (Accepted). This signals to the frontend that the job has been queued and is running in the background.

  3. CAP Logic calls Stored Procedure (Service -> Data): The CAP backend hands the heavy lifting off to the native HANA layer. This is done via a CALL proc (SQL/ODBC) that triggers the native HANA Stored Procedure (SQLScript).

  4. Native HANA Job Execution (Data -> External -> Data): The Stored Procedure executes the native job. It connects to external industrial APIs, fetches measurement data, processes it, and saves it into the HDI container’s Column Store. This can take seconds or minutes without blocking the UI.

  5. Job Completion & Event Push (Data -> Service) -> WebSocket Trigger: Once the HANA procedure finishes, it sends a status trigger back to the CAP service. The CAP handler then triggers the WebSocket event.

Below is the code snippet showing how the business logic triggers the event using the wsClients registry. In this example, we send a status update to the validation-status channel:

// srv/handlers/validationHandler.js (Example location)
const { wsClients } = require('../ws/wsRegistry'); // Import the registry

// ... Inside business logic, after successful job completion ...
const jobId = result.jobId; 
const final = true; 
const isValid = true; 

// Trigger wsSend with business payload
wsSend("validation-status", {
    jobId,
    status: final ? "SUCCESS" : "IN_PROGRESS",
    request_Id: result?.CheckRequest || result?.Request || result?.request || null,
    isValid: final ? isValid : null,
    detailStatus: final ? 200 : 202,
    resultStatus: final
      ? (isValid ? 'VALIDATED' : 'NOT_VALIDATED')
      : 'PENDING'
});

6.  'JobCompleted' Event Sent (Service -> Client): The Express WebSocket endpoint receives the trigger and uses the registry (wsClients['validation-status']) to actively push the JobCompleted event to the connected Angular UI via the WSS path.

Here is the core wsSend broadcasting method. Note how it cleans up closed connections automatically:

// srv/ws/wsBroadcaster.js
const { wsClients } = require('./wsRegistry');

async function wsSend(type, payload) {
  try {
    const clients = wsClients[type];
    const idx = Number(process.env.CF_INSTANCE_INDEX || process.env.INSTANCE_INDEX || 0);
    console.log(`[wsSend] broadcast on instance ${idx}, channel ${type}`);

    if (!clients || clients.size === 0) return false;

    const msg = JSON.stringify({
      event: 'statusEvent',
      data: { type, ...payload }
    });

    for (const ws of clients) {
      try {
        if (ws.readyState === ws.OPEN) {
          ws.send(msg); 
        } else {
          clients.delete(ws); // Cleanup
        }
      } catch (err) {
        clients.delete(ws);
      }
    }
    return true;
  } catch (err) {
    return false;
  }
}

The UI Reaction (Ensuring Consistency)

In response to the incoming JobCompleted event, the Angular UI performs an OData Refresh. This ensures the most recent data is pulled from the CAP service via the standard OData CRUD path, keeping the UI perfectly in sync.

Technical Notes: package.json Setup

To enable this in your CAP Node.js backend, you'll need express-ws. This allows you to integrate WebSockets directly into the Express app that CAP sits on:

{
  "dependencies": {
    "express-ws": "5.0.2",
    "express": "^4"
  }
}

Conclusion

By combining the productivity of SAP CAP with the raw power of HANA Stored Procedures and the push capabilities of WebSockets, we’ve built a resilient real-time architecture for on-premise industrial applications. This asynchronous workflow with HTTP 202 effectively kills gateway timeouts and ensures a fluid user experience.

Key Benefits:

  • Peak Performance: Native HANA procedures handle massive data imports without Node.js overhead.

  • Enhanced UX: Immediate 202 responses and real-time status updates prevent UI lockups.

  • Maintainability: A structured WebSocket registry allows for a clean separation of business channels.

  • Data Sovereignty: The entire stack remains securely on your on-premise HANA XSA infrastructure.

How do you handle mass data jobs and real-time requirements in your CAP apps? Are you already utilizing asynchronous patterns? I’d love to hear your thoughts in the comments!

entry.title: Implementing Dynamic Subaccount-Based Routing in SAP CAP with SAP BTP Destinations.
entry.content:
Introduction
When developing applications using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model(CAP) on SAP Business Technology Platform(BTP), we frequently connect to external systems such as SAP S/4HANA, CPI, or third party APIs.
Many developers initially use environment variables to store backend URLs and endpoint paths. While this works, it requires redeployment whenever an endpoint changes.
A more scalable and flexible approach is to use SAP BTP Cockpit Destinations, combined with dynamic instance or subaccount identification at runtime.

Why Not Use Environmental Variables.
Using environment variables means:

  • Endpoints must be manually maintained per environment
  • Application redeployment is required when URLs change
  • Configuration becomes complex in multi tenant scenarios
  • Credentials may risk exposure

Instead, SAP BTP Destinations provide:

  • Centralized configuration in the subaccount
  • Secure credential storage
  • Landscape independent deployment
  • Runtime flexibility

Creating and Binding the Destination Service.
Before consuming destinations, ensure the Destination service is added in your mta.yaml.
Example:-

 #Add this code in your mta.yaml file.
- name: destination-service
  type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
  parameters:
    service: destination
    service-plan: lite

you can refer the below screenshot.

Deploy the application on Cloud Foundry so the service binding is created.

Install required dependency:

npm install @sap-cloud-sdk/http-client

Import library:

const { executeHttpRequest } = require('@sap-cloud-sdk/http-client');

Run this command to test the destination service from the locally in SAP BAS:

cds bind -2 destination-service:myService

Where:

  • destination-service → Destination Instance name used in mta.yaml file.
  • myService → Service key name you can see this in destination-service under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

After deployment to Cloud Foundry, you can view Destination Service Instance bindings under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

Managing Endpoint Paths Better Approaches:
Option 1(Basic Approach): Maintain Mapping in Service Code.
You can dynamically identify the instance or subaccount using request headers.

Idea for the Origin and Naming Convention of the Subaccount:
The origin typically follows this format:

GlobalAccount_SubAccountName-InstanceId.launchpad.hana.ondemand.com

For example, if you are configuring a system for the ABC organization, you can name the subaccount using the client organization name along with the environment name.
Assume the environment is Development. In that case, the subaccount name can be:

development-ABC

Based on this naming convention, the origin will appear as:

GlobalAccount_development-ABC-devinstanceId.launchpad.hana.ondemand.com

Here, -ABC- acts as an identifier, which can be used to determine or retrieve the corresponding instance associated with that organization.

Example:-

// Retrive Instance Id from the Request header
const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';

const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

Important Notes:

  • In some cases, you may need to explicitly pass the origin header in the service request.
  • When creating subaccounts/instances, ensure a consistent naming convention for identifier across all environments (DEV, QA, UAT, PROD) it will help us to retrieve the Account Name/Instance ID best practice use Org_Name as identifier.
  • example Org Name = "GlobalAccountName_SubAccountName-InstanceId"
  • Keeping a constant identifier (i.e. organization short name) helps extract the instance ID reliably.

Instance Based Mapping:
Following is the example of the instance id's with respect to their endpoint URL. 

  • devinstanceId   → http/dev/path
  • iqainstanceId   → http/iqa/path
  • cqainstanceId   → http/cqa/path
  • uatinstanceId   → http/uat/path
  • prdinstanceId   → http/prod/path

Example:-

async function toCallCockpitDestination(req,payload) {
    try {
    let endPointUrl  = '';
    // Retrive Instance Id from the Request header
    const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';
    const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

    // compare Instance Id getting from the request with the Instance Id/Account Name.
    if(instanceId === 'devinstanceId'){  // DEV
        endPointUrl  = `http/dev/path`;
    } else if(instanceId === 'iqainstanceId'){ // IQa
        endPointUrl  = `http/iqa/path`;
    } else if (instanceId === 'cqainstanceId') { //CQA
        endPointUrl  = `http/cqa/path`;
    }else if (instanceId === 'prdinstanceId') { // Prod
        endPointUrl  = `http/prod/path`;
    }else{
        endPointUrl  ='http/dev/path'
    }
    // Call Destination service
    const getcmisresp = await executeHttpRequest({ destinationName: 'CockpitDestinatinName' }, { 
      method: 'POST',
      url: endPointUrl,
      headers: { 
        'Content-Type': 'application/json'           
      },
      data: payload                                 // CPI Email Body    
    },
      { fetchCsrfToken: false } // <--- Prevents HEAD for CSRF
    );
    // Return true false value based on the response status to confirm the execution.
    return (await getcmisresp).status === 201 ? true : false;

  } catch (error) {
      console.error('Error folder creation : ', error.response ? error.response.data : error.message);
    }
  }

This enables dynamic routing without environment variables.
However, this still hardcodes endpoints in the services which is not ideal.

Option 2 (Recommended): Store Endpoint Mapping in a Catalog Table and use in the SAP BTP Cockpit Destination call.
Instead of hardcoding endpoint paths inside the service, store them in a database table (.hdbtable).

Why This Is Better:
If the endpoint path changes:

  • You do NOT need to redeploy the CAP application
  • You only need to update the catalog table data
  • This is extremely helpful during last-minute delivery changes

This reduces deployment risk and improves operational flexibility.

Example Catalog Table Structure (ENDPOINTPATH.hdbtable):-

Subaccount_ID

Endpoint_Path

devinstanceId

http/dev/path

iqainstanceId

http/iqa/path

cqainstanceId

http/cqa/path

uatinstanceId

http/uat/path

prdinstanceId

http/prod/path

Get Instance/Subaccount ID from the request headers:

const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';
const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

Then retrieve it using a SQL query:

// Write a query to get Endpoint Path URL from the table based on the Instance Id/Subaccount Id
const query = await SELECT Endpoint_Path from ENDPOINTPATH where Subaccount_ID =  ?;
const result = await cds.run(query, [instanceId]);
const endPointUrl = result?.Endpoint_Path;

Now endpoint logic is externalized into the database.

Write a service to retrieve Endpoint URL from the catalog table and use in the SAP BTP Cockpit Destination call.
Example:-

async function toCallCockpitDestination(req,payload) {
  try {
    // Get instance ID from headers
    const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';
    const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

    // Retrieve endpoint from catalog table
    const query = `SELECT Endpoint_Path from "ENDPOINTPATH" where Subaccount_ID = ?`;
    const result = await cds.run(query, [instanceId]);
    const endPointUrl = result?.Endpoint_Path;

    // Call Destination service
    const getcmisresp = await executeHttpRequest({ destinationName: 'CockpitDestinatinName' }, { 
      method: 'POST',
      url: endPointUrl,
      headers: { 
        'Content-Type': 'application/json'           
      },
      data: payload                                 // CPI Email Body    
    },
      { fetchCsrfToken: false } // <--- Prevents HEAD for CSRF
    );
    // Return true/false value based on the response status to confirm the execution.
    return (await getcmisresp).status === 201 ? true : false;

  } catch (error) {
      console.error('Error folder creation : ', error.response ? error.response.data : error.message);
    }
}

Testing tip's locally in SAP Business Application Studio.
If you are running the application locally in SAP Business Application Studio, ensure that the SAP BTP Destination Service is bound to the application so that the destination can be resolved correctly.

Run this command to test the destination service from the locally in SAP BAS:

cds bind -2 destination-service:myService

Where:

  • destination-service → Destination Instance name used in mta.yaml file.
  • myService → Service key name you can see this in destination-service under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

After deployment to Cloud Foundry, you can view Destination Service Instance bindings under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

How to use this service.

  • We have created the async function and export it from the same file. This allows the function to be reused anywhere in the project by importing it into other files or functions.
  • The function can also be called separately from the service layer (  .js and  .cds files). Import the function and pass the required payload and request headers as parameters.

you can refer the below screenshot for how to export async function.

Final Architecture Outcome
Now:

  • Endpoint logic is stored in a catalog table
  • Connection configuration is managed in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • No environment variables are required
  • Endpoint changes require only table updates
  • No full application redeployment is needed

Benefits of This Approach

  • Centralized configuration in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • No environment variable management
  • Dynamic multi tenant routing
  • Reduced redeployment risk
  • Faster last minute delivery adjustments
  • Clean separation of configuration and business logic

Conclusion
Using SAP BTP Cockpit Destinations in CAP projects significantly improves endpoint management and operational flexibility.
By dynamically retrieving the instance/subaccount ID and storing endpoint mappings in a catalog table, you can avoid hardcoding URLs and eliminate unnecessary redeployments.
This approach is especially valuable in multi-tenant SaaS applications running on SAP BTP, where flexibility, security, and maintainability are essential.

entry.title: Automate BTP Custom Domain certificates using Let's Encrypt
entry.content:
Part II of my BTP Toolbox: This application brings fully automated certificate management to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. It combines a CAP backend, a Fiori Elements UI, SAP Custom Domain service integration, Cloud Foundry route handling, and Job Scheduler to manage the complete lifecycle of certificates for custom domains using Let’s Encrypt. Instead of handling certificate requests, renewals, uploads, activation, and cleanup manually, teams can operate the whole process through one application with a transparent user interface and scheduled background jobs.

What It Is Good For

  • Reducing manual effort in certificate operations for SAP BTP custom domains
  • Preventing outages caused by expired certificates
  • Giving operations teams a controlled and visible process instead of script-heavy administration
  • Supporting recurring certificate renewal without relying on manual reminders
  • Managing certificate operations in landscapes where apps and domains are distributed across spaces or regions

The whole certificate process is quite heavy and consists of multiple manual steps documented in SAP Help.

This application automates highlighted recurring tasks on BTP after the initial setup like CNAME mapping (DNS) and registering the top-level-domain:

Highlights

  • End-to-end certificate lifecycle automation using Let's Encrypt ACME challenge
  • Support for creating CSRs, requesting certificates, uploading chains, and activating them
  • Automated renewal of active certificates through scheduled jobs
  • Safe cleanup of expired certificates only when a valid replacement already exists
  • Fiori-based operational UI for certificate actions and scheduler administration
  • Integration with SAP Custom Domain service, Cloud Foundry APIs, Destination service, and Job Scheduler
  • Support for staging mode to test flows safely before consuming production Let’s Encrypt limits
  • Designed to work in SAP BTP Extension Landscapes, with one deployment per region

Github Repository: https://github.com/bkrencker/letsencrypt 

Feel free to use this project, submit pull requests, start discussions about ideas and submit issues on Github.

First article in my BTP Toolbox: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-toolbox-app-to-backup-amp-restore-hdi-containers/ba-p/13703879

entry.title: Running Hugging Face AI Models with SAPUI5 and CAPM
entry.content:
Hi everyone !

After spending several hours researching and experimenting, I finally found a simple way to answer a question that many SAP developers have started asking:

  “Is it possible to run AI models inside SAP applications using SAPUI5 and CAPM?”

The short answer is:

   Yes, absolutely!

In this blog, I will share how we can integrate Hugging Face AI models with SAPUI5 and CAPM, and how we can build a simple AI-powered application that can generate text responses and even images.

First Question: What is Hugging Face?

 If you are new to AI development, you might wonder:

What exactly is Hugging Face?

Think of Hugging Face as GitHub for AI models.

It is a platform where developers and researchers publish and share machine learning models.

Currently, Hugging Face provides access to hundreds of thousands of AI models that can perform tasks such as:

  • Text generation
  • Chatbots
  • Image generation
  • Speech recognition
  • Translation
  • Code generation

The best part is that you can use these models directly through APIs without training your own model.

Another great advantage is that Hugging Face provides GPU infrastructure, which means you can run advanced models without owning expensive hardware.

And yes — if you build your own model, you can also publish it on Hugging Face and make it available globally.

        Fig 1.1 Hugging Face Models sections

 

Now the Interesting Part: Can SAPUI5 Use AI?

When I started this experiment, my first question was:

Can SAPUI5 directly call an AI model?

The answer is yes, especially for text generation models.

To make this work, we only need three things:

-  A Hugging Face account
-  An HF API Token
-  A model endpoint

Once you log in to Hugging Face, you can generate an HF Token from your account settings.

Then you can choose any model depending on your use case. Hugging Face provides model benchmarks and descriptions, so you can easily decide which one is best for:

  • Education
  • Coding
  • Reasoning
  • Chat
  • Content generation

Connecting Hugging Face with SAPUI5

Now let's talk about the implementation.

In the SAPUI5 controller, we can send a request to the Hugging Face API using JavaScript fetch().

The UI simply takes a user prompt, sends it to the AI model, and displays the response.

Example flow:

User enters a prompt:

       "What is Hugging Face and SAPUI5?"

SAPUI5 sends the prompt to the Hugging Face model.

The model generates a response and returns it.

Then SAPUI5 displays the result directly on the UI.

This makes it very easy to build AI chat assistants inside SAP applications.

         Fig 1.2 Output- Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

Sample Code:

      Fig 1.3 sapui5 Controller.js - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

        Fig 1.3 sapui5 view.xml - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

But What About Image Generation?

This is where things become interesting.

When I tried running image generation models directly in SAPUI5, I realized something important.

⚠️SAPUI5 works best with text-based AI APIs.

For image generation models, we need a backend service.

This is where CAPM (Cloud Application Programming Model) becomes very useful.

 

 

Using CAPM for Image Generation

To generate images, we can integrate fal.ai, which provides inference APIs for advanced image generation models.

Some examples include:

  • black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev
  • Tongyi-MAI / Z-Image-Turbo

These models can generate high-quality AI images from text prompts.

For example:

"Portrait of a futuristic astronaut with glowing blue eyes"

The system sends this prompt to the AI model, and the model generates an image.

 

How CAPM Handles This

Inside the CAPM Node.js service, we create a custom action such as:

     "generateImage"

This action receives the prompt from SAPUI5.

Then CAPM calls the fal.ai API with that prompt.

The AI model generates the image and returns a URL of the generated image, which we send back to the UI.

This makes CAPM act as a bridge between SAPUI5 and AI models.

 

Why Use fal.ai Instead of Hugging Face Directly?

You might ask:

       If we already use Hugging Face, why do we need fal.ai?

          Good question.

Some models listed on Hugging Face are actually hosted through different inference providers, including fal.ai.

For example:

black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev

This model is often executed through fal.ai inference APIs.

So fal.ai becomes an easy way to run those models with strong GPU performance.

Sample Code:

 

Fig 1.4 Output – CAPM - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.5 service.js - first half- Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.6 service.js - second half - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.7 Fal.ai API key Generation - Hugging face Image generation

 

Final Result

After connecting everything together, we get a simple AI application:

The application can:

- Generate AI text responses using SAPUI5
- Generate AI images using CAPM services
- Use Hugging Face models
- Run without training your own model

All of this can be done using free developer APIs, unless you need large-scale GPU workloads.

 

Conclusion

From this experiment, we can see that SAP applications can easily integrate with modern AI platforms.

The architecture becomes very simple:

                   SAPUI5
                        ⬇
           CAPM Service
                      ⬇
   Hugging Face / fal.ai AI Models

 

Using this approach, developers can build powerful applications such as:

  • AI chat assistants inside SAP
  • AI-powered knowledge systems
  • Image generation tools
  • Smart enterprise automation

And the best part is that you can even deploy your own AI model on Hugging Face and use it inside SAP applications.

 

I hope this small experiment helps other developers who are curious about AI + SAP integration.

Happy coding!

entry.title: A Use Case for HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph: AI‑Driven Tender Analysis
entry.content:
Introduction

Many companies face the same challenge: they need to match unstructured text, such as tenders, applications, or support tickets, with their internal data to make informed decisions. In our project, we tackled exactly this problem and worked on an application that connects text understanding, semantic search and structured data. Our primary objective was to acquire knowledge about the tools SAP offers for developing applications of this nature. We undertook this project as part of SAP’s Early Adopter Care program for the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine.

Our concrete use case was an internal tender analysis scenario within our company, focusing on public-sector calls for tenders. Deciding about potential participation requires answering several questions: Does the topic fit our company's portfolio, which skills and experience are required and do the required skills exist in our organization, are the necessary resources available? Instead of manually searching for this information through documents and databases, we wanted to automate this process. The goal was to build a system that can read and interpret the tender text, understand the company's internal skill profiles, and combine both to produce a meaningful recommendation.

To achieve this, we combined three capabilities:

  • semantic structuring of employee data using a knowledge graph
  • semantic representation of competency descriptions and CVs using vector embeddings
  • LLM-powered analysis to interpret and connect both worlds

This architecture allowed us to bring together unstructured text and structured data enabling precise matching and explainable results, thus building a foundation that can be reused for many other AI scenarios.

We implemented the application using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), a cloud-native framework that seamlessly integrates with SAP HANA Cloud, SAP AI Core and the Generative AI Hub (GenAI Hub). To give you a clear picture of how these components work together, the solution diagram below illustrates the overall flow, from data ingestion to semantic processing to the final application.

Solution Diagram

Provisioning the Resources

To implement this architecture, we provisioned a set of cloud resources in SAP BTP , each of which contribute a specific capability to the overall workflow.

At the center of the setup is SAP HANA Cloud, which provides both the Knowledge Graph Engine (KGE) and the Vector Engine (VE), and in which the CAP application data is persisted. The KGE is not enabled by default; it must be activated when the HANA instance is created and comes with some technical requirements:

  • Triple Store activation: The KGE relies on the HANA Triple Store, which must be enabled as part of the instance configuration. Without this, RDF triples cannot be stored or queried.
  • Minimum memory requirement: The KGE requires a HANA instance with at least 48 GB memory. This is necessary because storing the graph, executing SPARQL and SQL statements, and inference processes such as subclass inference, domain/range inference, and transitive property expansion operate in-memory. Smaller instances cannot activate Triple Store at all.

For the AI workflows, we provisioned AI Core which includes the GenAI Hub. AI Core runs the embedding and LLM processing jobs, while the GenAI Hub provides access to the models used for these tasks. This allows the application to generate semantic representations of text documents (tenders and CVs in our use case) and to retrieve information of documents via prompts.

The application layer itself is deployed as a CAP service running in a Cloud Foundry environment. CAP binds to the HANA Cloud, AI Core, and GenAI Hub service instances through standard service bindings, i.e. by receiving all required credentials and endpoints through environment variables.

This provisioning model keeps the architecture modular: each capability (knowledge graph, vector search, AI processing, and application logic) is provided as a separate cloud resource. It also makes the system easy to extend.

Knowledge Graphs and Embeddings: The Semantic Foundation of Our Architecture

Before diving into the SAP-specific components, it is useful to look at the two core concepts that shape our solution: knowledge graphs and embeddings. Both address the same challenge from different angles: turning human-readable meaning into structured, machine-interpretable representation.

Knowledge Graphs: Representing Relations between Data Entities

A knowledge graph stores information not as tables, but as entities and relationships. Instead of using foreign keys and joins, it forms an explicit network of connected facts. This makes it easier to express complex relationships, integrate data, and run queries that follow semantic structures rather than rigid schemas.

At the heart of every knowledge graph is an ontology: a structured definition of entities, properties, and relationships that the graph represents. An ontology defines:

  • classes (e.g., Person, Skill, Project)
  • properties that connect them (e.g., hasSkill, requiresSkill)
  • hierarchies and subclasses (e.g., abstract classes or hierarchical organization units)
  • constraints and expected value types (e.g., each identifiable entity has exactly one ID of type int)

The ontology gives the graph its semantic structure. It tells the system what kinds of things exist in the domain and how they relate to each other. Without an ontology, a knowledge graph would be just a collection of disconnected triples.

Knowledge graphs are typically stored as RDF (Resource Description Framework) triples, with the following general structure:

Subject – Predicate – Object
  • Subject: the thing we are talking about
  • Predicate: the property or relationship
  • Object: the value or another entity

For example:

Person123 – hasSkill – Python

This triple structure is simple but flexible and extensible. It is especially suitable for representing domains where the data model evolves over time.

To query RDF data, we use SPARQL, a graph-oriented query language. Instead of specifying joins explicitly – as is required in SQL – SPARQL expresses patterns that should appear in the data. This approach is particularly powerful for dynamic or AI-generated queries, where the structure of the query may not be known in advance. While SQL’s traditional query optimization excels when working with predefined schemas and foreign keys, it becomes less flexible when relationships or entity types vary. SPARQL, by contrast, is built to operate on semantic relationships and can naturally accommodate evolving graph structures.

The conceptual difference becomes clear when comparing a typical SQL query—which depends on rigid tables and join conditions—with an equivalent SPARQL query, where relationships are expressed in a way that is closer to how users understand the domain. In SPARQL, entities and relationships are simply assumed to exist in the graph without requiring predefined foreign-key constraints.

SQL-Query example

SELECT
    u.UserID,
    u.JobPosition,
    u.OrgUnitID,
    s.SkillName,
    us.SkillLevel,
    us.SkillLevelName,
FROM Users u
JOIN UserSkills us ON u.UserID = us.UserID
JOIN Skills s ON us.SkillID = s.SkillID
WHERE u.Active = 1

SPARQL-Query example

PREFIX : <http://example.org/my-project#>
PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
PREFIX xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#>

SELECT ?person ?position ?skill ?skillName ?level
WHERE {
    ?person rdf:type :Person ;
            :isActive true ;
            :hasJobPosition ?position ;
            :hasUserSkill ?userSkill .
    ?userSkill :connectsToSkill ?skill ;
               :hasSkillLevel ?level ;
               :hasSkillLevelName ?levelName .
    ?skill :hasSkillName ?skillName .   
}

 In short:

  • SQL is designed for handling stable, well-defined tabular schemas with fixed relationships.
  • SPARQL is designed for handling semantic relationships, flexible graph structures, and dynamically changing query shapes, making it well suited for AI-generated scenarios.

Embeddings: Encoding Text Semantics as Vectors

Embeddings take a different approach. Instead of modelling explicit relationships, they encode the semantic meaning of text as high-dimensional vectors. Similar texts end up close to each other in vector space while unrelated texts are far apart. This enables semantic search, similarity matching and clustering. Embeddings are essential when working with documents like tenders or CVs, where meaning is not explicitly structured but must be inferred.

How This Maps to the SAP World: KGE and VE

SAP HANA Cloud provides built-in components that implement these concepts directly inside the database:

  • The Knowledge Graph Engine (KGE) stores RDF triples and executes SPARQL queries. It gives us explicit, queryable relationships between entities such as people, skills, and projects.
  • The Vector Engine (VE) stores embeddings and provides functions for calculating the cosine similarity between embeddings, which simplifies similarity searches. It allows us to compare tender documents with CVs or skill profiles on a semantic level.

Together, KGE and VE combine explicit knowledge in the form of the knowledge graph structure with implicit meaning in the form of text embeddings. This dual representation enables our application to extract relevant information from tender documents and map it to our data model of employee competencies.

In the next section, we will look at how we designed the ontology and processed the data to populate both engines.

Building the Knowledge Graph

Creating the knowledge graph was a multi-step process that combined data engineering, domain modeling, and graph construction. While some parts of the pipeline can be automated, the core design work requires domain knowledge and deliberate modeling decisions. In our project we followed a three-stage approach.

Selecting and Structuring Relevant Data

The first step was to identify which data should become part of the knowledge graph and how these pieces of information relate to each other. This is a classic data engineering task: understanding the domain, exploring the available data sources, and deciding which entities and relationships are relevant for the scenario.

In our case, this meant analyzing the existing SQL-based data model (employees, skills, projects, roles, and assignments) and determining which parts of it contain meaningful information for matching tenders with internal capabilities. This step cannot be easily automated; it requires domain knowledge to decide which entities and relationships between them matter for the business question and which attributes are relevant for which entity type.

Creating An Ontology

Once the structure of the ontology has been decided on, it can be translated into classes, properties, hierarchies, and constraints that structure the graph.

Our ontology is defined using Turtle (Terse RDF Triple Language), a human-readable syntax for expressing RDF data. Turtle is a W3C standard that provides a compact and intuitive way to describe semantic relationships. It is based on the following key syntax elements:

Prefixes: Define namespace shortcuts for cleaner code

@prefix : <http://example.org/our-project#> .
@prefix owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .

Classes: Define entity types using a owl:Class

:Person a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "Person" ;
    rdfs:comment "Individual employee or consultant" .

Properties: Define relationships (ObjectProperty) or attributes (DatatypeProperty)

:possessesSkillWith a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:domain :Person ;
    rdfs:range :UserSkill .

This links a person to their skill instances which include, e.g., proficiency levels and experience.

Triples: The fundamental RDF structure (Subject-Predicate-Object)

:Person rdfs:subClassOf :IdentifiableEntity .

This reads as: "Person is a subclass of IdentifiableEntity"

The semicolon notation allows defining multiple properties of the same entity without repeating the subject. The following statement is equivalent to four separate triples, but much more readable:

:Person a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "Person" ;
    rdfs:comment "Individual employee" ;
    rdfs:subClassOf :IdentifiableEntity .

To organize related concepts, we introduced abstract classes. They reduce redundancy by collecting shared properties in one place instead of repeating them across many similar classes. This makes the ontology easier to maintain and ensures consistency because updates to shared properties only need to be made once.

In addition to structuring the model, the ontology also defines formal constraints that describe how data in the knowledge graph is expected to look. These constraints specify rules and cardinalities, such as requiring each person to have exactly one UserID or ensuring that each project includes a start date. While SAP HANA Cloud cannot automatically enforce these constraints without a reasoner, they still serve as valuable documentation and help implementing validation checks using Shapes Constraint Language tools.

Creating Instances

The knowledge graph contains instances that are generated from data stored in a relational database. To connect both worlds, we defined a mapping that links SQL tables and columns to ontology properties. For example, the ontology property hasUserID corresponds to the column UserID in the table Users in an SQL database. This declarative mapping makes it possible to extract relational data and convert it into semantic triples .

Simple one‑to‑one and one‑to‑many relationships can be handled directly. For many‑to‑many relationships, we added an extended mapping layer that can follow junction tables in the relational schema.

Because SAP HANA Cloud’s RDF Store does not include a reasoner, inverse properties cannot be inferred automatically. They must be created explicitly during triple generation. To support this, our mapping configuration allows us to define inverse property pairs (e.g., tagsProject ↔ projectTaggedWith), so the parser generates both directions of the relationship automatically.

The triple generation pipeline runs in several steps. First, it creates datatype property triples (literal attributes), automatically assigning the correct types based on the ontology. Then it generates object property triples (links between entities), using an in-memory index to quickly look up target URIs. For large datasets, the system uses chunked batch processing to load triples in bulk, which improves performance and reduces memory usage.

Each ontology class is written into its own RDF graph, allowing updates to specific entity types without reloading the entire knowledge base. Finally, the system serializes these graphs into Turtle (.ttl) files and uploads them to the SAP Knowledge Graph Engine (i.e., SAP HANA Cloud’s RDF Store).

Requirement Analysis and Evaluation

Extracting required skills and experience levels via Prompts

To extract information from tender documents, we developed an LLM‑based pipeline that first checks whether a tender fits the company’s domain. For tenders that match the business profile, the pipeline automatically generates a concise text summary and extracts the skills referenced in the document. For each identified skill, it captures the skill name, and its stated importance level in the tender — classified as must-have, should-have, or nice-to-have (when explicitly indicated in the text).

Skills and priorities

Skill Matching

This structured extraction turns free‑text requirements into machine‑readable skill profiles that can be directly queried within our knowledge graph. For these queries, the system needs to match the skills extracted by the LLM with the skill names stored in the employee database. These names rarely match exactly because of synonyms or different wording. We have implemented this matching by first looking up the top-k skills most similar in terms of word embedding using the Knowledge Graph Engine and subsequently filtering out entries that are unrelated to the skill mentioned in the tender via a prompt (for example, matching “Python” in the tender to entries like “Python 3” or “Python Programming” in the employee database).

Once the skill mapping is complete, the matched skills are inserted into a predefined SPARQL query. This query returns all employees who have these skills, including their proficiency levels, enabling automated candidate identification for tender requirements.

By combining embeddings with LLMs, the system can handle linguistic variations while still generating precise SPARQL queries.

Users matching

 

Requirement Evaluation

Once employees with the required skills are found through the SPARQL queries, the system calculates a skill score based on their proficiency levels. A skill receives a score of 1.0 if at least one employee is an expert, 0.8 for intermediate proficiency, 0.5 for beginner-level proficiency, and 0.0 if no one in the company has that skill. This gives an overall picture of how well the organization covers each required competency.

The skill scores are aggregated within the importance levels (must‑have, should‑have, nice‑to‑have) using a simple average. The result is a score for each importance level that shows how well the company’s employees' competencies match the tender requirements at different levels of importance.

Aggregated scores

Supplementary Analysis: Comparing Employee CVs and Tender Text

Beyond the structured skill matching outlined above, we added a second analysis pipeline which determines the suitability of candidates for a given tender document by evaluating the semantic similarity between employee CVs and tender text based on embeddings.

Employee CVs are split into meaningful chunks (up to 6,000 tokens each) so that related information about projects and skills stays together. Each chunk is then embedded using a suitable model deployed through the SAP AI Core and stored in SAP Vector Engine (i.e. SAP HANA Cloud’s vector database) for semantic search. Tender documents go through the same embedding process, turning unstructured text into dense vectors that capture meaning beyond simple keywords.

To identify suitable candidates, the system compares CV embeddings with tender embeddings using cosine similarity, ranking employees according to how closely their experience aligns with the project requirements. By capturing semantic similarity rather than relying on exact keyword matches, this approach enables accurate matching even when different wording or terminology is used.

By combining precise skill matching through the knowledge graph with semantic search across CV embeddings, the system provides a comprehensive way to find candidates based both on explicitly listed competencies and experience descriptions.

Outlook

As a next step, we plan to enable fully automated tender analysis where LLMs generate SPARQL queries directly from the tender text, without relying on predefined templates. This means the LLM would translate project requirements into precise knowledge graph queries on its own. For example, if a tender asks for “three senior Java developers with cloud architecture experience and at least five years in the financial sector,” the LLM would automatically create a SPARQL query that filters employees by skill levels, domain experience, and project history, returning all candidates who meet these criteria in one step.

entry.title: SAP Developer News, March 19th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS 

ABAP Developer Challenge – halfway point! 

  • ABAP Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers using Basic Trial (blog post) https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/abap-developer-challenge-joule-for-developers-using-basic-trial/ba-p/14336191 

SAP Insider Las Vegas 

  • Event website https://vegas.sapinsider.org/  

Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition 

  • Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition (blog post) https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/introducing-sap-integration-suite-enhanced-edition/ba-p/14346638  

CAP Feb 2026 release 

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/feb26  

Feedback for the btp CLI and Terraform providers 

  • Post with links to both surveys https://www.linkedin.com/posts/christian-lechner-inthecloud_do-you-use-the-btp-cli-andor-terraform-activity-7435231498376482816-rjWW/  

DYK #8 Orchestration Client in SAP Cloud SDK for AI 

  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI – Chat Completion documentation https://sap.github.io/ai-sdk/docs/js/orchestration/chat-completion  

CHAPTER TITLES  

00:00 Intro 
00:07 ABAP Developer Challenge – halfway point! 
00:39 SAP Insider Las Vegas 
01:59 Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition  
03:10 CAP Feb 2026 release 
04:58 Feedback for the btp CLI and Terraform providers  
05:56 DYK #8 Orchestration Client in SAP Cloud SDK for AI 

TRANSCRIPT

This is the SAP Developer News for March 19th, 2026.

[Sheena]
Hello, everyone. We are halfway through the March Developer Challenge on Joule
ABAP AI capabilities. And we would like to thank all of you for the amazing
responses we have been receiving for the past two weeks. As you might know,
week three is already live and we are focusing on RAP predict business logic to
create a sample code by passing a simple prompt. We have one more week
remaining and we are excited to hear even more great responses. Till then, stay
Jouled!

[Rich and Witalij]
Hey folks, it's Rich Heilman and Witalij here in Las Vegas for SAP Insider
2026. Today we did a two-hour hands-on session on Joule Studio, which was good.
Lots of good questions and tomorrow we're going to repeat that and we're going
to do a Joule for Developers in SAP Build and a Joule for Developers ABAP
edition as well where we'll show everyone how to use Joule to speed up their
development process. And you did a hands-on session this morning. Yes and I did
a session on how to use SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine Capabilities to build the
Joule augmented generation. Lots of people are talking about agents right now
but a lot of customers are doing RAG based AI applications so it was good to
refresh this content and as well to mention that we still keep investing in
some of the capabilities in SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine like for example the
very recent new encoder model. Cool very cool yeah so we got a couple more
hands-on sessions and I think we're gonna get out here and get on to the next
one. So, bye for now.

[Antonio]
Hola, SAP developers. There is a new edition available for SAP Integration
Suite, which includes lots of goodies. This is the new Enhanced Edition. From
an AI perspective, it includes features that were only previously available in
the Premium Edition. For example, iFlow generation using natural language,
script optimization to analyze, refactor, and optimize Groovy or JavaScript
scripts, API anomaly detection, and API traffic prediction to help you manage
your APIs. Apart from that, you also get access to the entire suite of
integration capabilities, an advanced event mesh broker for your event-driven
architecture scenarios, SAP Document AI to turn unstructured data into
structured data, the alert notification service for monitoring, transport
management service to move artifacts across landscapes without manual exports.
So, lots of stuff. Make sure to check out Tricia's blog post on SAP Community
to get all the details. Hasta luego. Hello, everyone.

[Ajay]
The February release of Capire documentation (*CAP) is out. First, we have an
exciting update. Live queries in the documentation. Yes, you have heard that
right. You can now run CDS queries directly in your browser. You can view the
query results along with the corresponding SQL statements. You can also edit
the query by typing in the box, making it your own personal playground to
experiment with, and no setups required. Coming to Node.js updates. Parallel
GET calls are now supported in the OData $batch request. For draft-enabled
entities, calculated elements can now be reliably used for values display in
the UI or for information in the UI behavior. Starting with @cap-js/sqlite
version Node.js SQL implementation by setting cds.requires.db.driver to node.
Coming to Java updates, important changes include using a reference as a value
for substring prefix or suffix in the contains startsWith or endsWith functions
is now rejected. Performance improvements have been made for requests related
to Fiori Draft list report "All" filter, deletion of inactive drafts during the
draft activation, and hierarchical selects. Tooling updates includes a new
query mode in cds REPL is available via the .ql command. CAP now supports
version 10 of ESLint. And for the Cloud Foundry, cds up can now be adjusted
using MTA extensions. For more details, visit the Capire documentation. Links
will be included in the description.

[DJ]
If you use the SAP Business Technology Platform, and more specifically, if
you're using the btp CLI or the Terraform providers to help you manage your
resources there, then your feedback is sought. The team has a couple of
feedback surveys open right now, running through April as well, so it's still a
little bit of time. One feedback survey for the btp CLI and the other for the
Terraform providers. This is a great opportunity for you to provide your
opinions and share with the team how you're using the tools and also where you
see room for improvement. Links to both surveys, which, as I mentioned, are
running sort of through April, are in the description. After this news episode
finishes, why not grab a coffee and head over and give your opinion right now.
Thanks.

[Kevin]
Did you know that you can configure an AI orchestration flow in AI Launchpad
and use it to configure the orchestration client from the SAP Cloud SDK for AI
within a CAP project? It allows you to configure templating, content filtering,
data masking, grounding, and more. You can download the configuration and read
it from file into your orchestration client or directly paste the JSON string
from AI Launchpad into your project. At any point, you can upload the
configuration back to AI Launchpad to make changes or directly change the
config inline of your project.

entry.title: Integrating a Custom AI Model into SAP CAP Using AI Launchpad
entry.content:
As we all know AI is spreading across industries like wildfire and a lot of companies want to bring their own custom models into their SAP setups. In this blog I will walk you through a hands-on example of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core. And then making it a callable service in SAP AI Launchpad and connecting it directly to a CAP backend.

Rather than relying on pre-built generative AI services, we demonstrate how to bring your own model, package it in Docker, register it in AI Launchpad, and call it from your backend using a deployment ID. The goal is to cover the whole journey from serving the model to getting real-time results right in your UI.

Requirements

If you are not running everything locally, you'll need admin roles for SAP AI Launchpad and AI Core. For a local setup, there's a build step. For running the trained model in Docker a GPU is recommended but not strictly mandatory. (A CPU will work, just slower) Make sure your machine has enough CPU power, memory and disk space. If you want GPU acceleration, you’ll need a compatible NVIDIA GPU, the right drivers, and CUDA support.

Getting Data Set For Model Training

You can grab image datasets from Roboflow, Kaggle, Google Open Images, or other publicly available sources. But if you plan to use your model for more than just experimenting, you have to check the licenses and usage rights carefully. Or just make your own dataset by collecting and labeling images for your actual use case. That way you’ve got control over both quality and compliance.

Running the Application Locally

The application is first run locally to verify that it works as expected after pushing the model to Docker.

The Dockerfile used for local testing

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

In the CAP application, the requires section in package.json is updated as follows:

"cds": {
  "sql": {
  },
  "requires": {
    "[production]": {
    },
    "[hybrid]": {
    },
    "barcode-ai": {
      "kind": "rest",
      "credentials": {
        "url": "http://localhost:8080",
        "requestTimeout": 300000
      }
    }
  },
  "auth": "xsuaa",
  "html5-runtime": true,
  "portal": true
},

On the UI side, users will upload barcode images through a FileUploader component.

<u:FileUploader
                    id="imageUploader"
                    name="myFileUpload"
                    fileType="jpg,jpeg,png"
                    placeholder="{i18n>selectBarcodeImg}"
                    change="onImageSelected"
                    width="300px" />

                <Button
                    text="{i18n>barcodeImgTrigger}"
                    type="Emphasized"
                    press="onAnalyzeBarcode"
                    class="sapUiMediumMarginTop" />

The controller then adds the uploaded image to a FormData object and fires off a POST request.

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Test: Please Select An Image");
        return;
      }

      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const formData = new FormData();
        formData.append("image", this._selectedFile);

        const response = await fetch(
          "/catalog/RoleSearchService/analyze-barcode",
          {
            method: "POST",
            body: formData
          }
        );

        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Test: Cant Call Ai Srv");
        }

        const result = await response.json();
        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          JSON.stringify(result, null, 2)
        );
      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Test: Barcode Srv Failed");
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

For local testing the service call in service.js points to your locally running API. With this end-to-end local test you can check if the API can spot barcodes in uploaded images. And if so, pull out their locations, values, types and confidence scores.

The request-response structure of the local test service

 

AI Launchpad Connections

Once you've pushed your project that includes the Dockerfile, serve.py, requirements.txt, and serving_template.yaml to the GitHub, head over to SAP AI Launchpad.

Application settings in AI Launchpad for local testing

Under SAP AI Core Administration, go to Git Repositories and add your GitHub URL. Once the repository is synced, open All Applications and then create a new application. Pick your repository, set the path to (.), and revision to (main).

With the application synced, move to ML Operations and open Scenarios. You will see the serving_template.yaml is picked up automatically as an executable. The template defines the Docker image to run, the container port, resource requests, and the imagePullSecret pointing to our Docker Hub credentials, which are registered separately under Docker Registry Secrets.

 

 

Next, make a Configuration under the scenario, linking the executable to a resource group.

One important detail here is that the serving_template.yaml must define the metadata annotations, labels, and spec fields as pipe-delimited strings rather than nested YAML objects. This is a requirement of the SAP AI Core ServingTemplate schema that differs from standard Kubernetes manifests.

apiVersion: ai.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: ServingTemplate
metadata:
  name: barcode-reader-serving
  namespace: <your-namespace>
  labels:
    scenarios.ai.sap.com/id: "barcode-scenario"
    ai.sap.com/version: "1.0.0"
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        autoscaling.knative.dev/metric: rps
        autoscaling.knative.dev/target: "100"
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: kserve-container
          image: "<your-dockerhub-username>/barcode-reader:latest"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
              protocol: TCP
          command: ["uvicorn", "serve:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "0.5"
              memory: "512Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1Gi"

 

Once your configuration is set trigger a Deployment. Use the infer.m resource plan, as it gives enough compute. After a few minutes the deployment will reach Running status with a unique Deployment ID.

Deployment Details in SAP AI Launchpad

 


Model binding in the updated package.json

Take that Deployment ID and put it into the cds.requires section of package.json under the barcode-ai destination. Now you can pass base64-encoded images in the request and get back barcode predictions from your CAP service layer.

 

 

 

 

 

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Please choose an image first!");
        return;
      }
      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const arrayBuffer = await this._selectedFile.arrayBuffer();
        const base64 = btoa(
          new Uint8Array(arrayBuffer)
            .reduce((data, byte) => data + String.fromCharCode(byte), '')
        );
        const response = await fetch("/catalog/SIAService/analyzeBarcodeImage", {
          method: "POST",
          headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
          body: JSON.stringify({ image: base64 })
        });
        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Error in Ai srv call");
        }
        const raw = await response.json();
        const result = typeof raw.value === 'string' ? JSON.parse(raw.value) : raw.value;

        if (!result.barcodeValue) {
          this.byId("resultText").setText("No barcode detected in the image");
          return;
        }

        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          `Barcode: ${result.barcodeValue} | Type: ${result.confidence || "-"}`
        );

        MessageToast.show("Barcode Scanned Successfully!");

      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Barcode Analysis Failed: " + err.message);
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

 

this.on('analyzeBarcodeImage', async (req) => {
      try {
        const image = req.data.image;
        if (!image) {
          req.error(400, 'Image is required');
          return;
        }

        const barcodeAI = await cds.connect.to('<Your_Destination>');
        const deploymentId = '<deploymentId>';
        const response = await barcodeAI.send({
          method: 'POST',
          path: `/v2/inference/deployments/${deploymentId}/v2/models/barcode-model/infer`,
          headers: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'AI-Resource-Group': 'default'
          },
          data: {
            image: image
          }
        });

        // response.predictions → [{data, type, rect}]
        const predictions = response?.predictions || [];

        if (predictions.length === 0) {
          return JSON.stringify({ barcodeValue: null, confidence: null, message: 'Barcode not found' });
        }

        return JSON.stringify({
          barcodeValue: predictions[0].data,
          confidence: predictions[0].type,
          allResults: predictions
        });

      } catch (err) {
        console.error('analyzeBarcodeImage error:', err);
        req.error(500, 'Barcode inference failed');
      }
    });

 

Once we have everything wired up and the CAP app is running, we head back to the frontend and run our test by uploading two images, one with a barcode and one without.

Barcode Detection In Action Test1

Barcode Detection In Action Test2

Future Improvements

The big takeaway integrating custom models with AI Launchpad is straightforward. And once you’ve plugged it in, you can use it across all kinds of projects with remarkable ease. That smooth integration makes a huge difference.

You can level this up in a bunch of ways. For example fine-tune a model like YOLOv8 for tricky conditions like dim lighting or angled shots or add QR code support with minimal changes or handle damaged barcodes by searching for similar visual embeddings in a database.

Summary

This blog walked through the full integration path of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core and consuming it from a CAP backend. Instead of relying on a managed generative AI service, the model was packaged in Docker, registered in SAP AI Launchpad via a serving template, and called from the CAP backend using a deployment ID over the AI Launchpad Destination.

The result is a working barcode detection feature that runs entirely on SAP infrastructure, with the model hosted and scaled by AI Core and the business logic kept cleanly within the CAP service layer. And as we covered, this is just the start. The architecture is flexible. Fine-tune for complex real world scenarios, tack on QR codes, or deal with scenarios like damaged barcodes using similarity search.

Hopefully, this gives consultants a clear roadmap for moving past generic AI tools and plugging their own models into real world solutions. You're not limited to barcodes either. This blueprint works for custom vision models, language models, whatever you need.

If you have any questions or thoughts, feel free to leave a comment below.

Ege Aksöyek

entry.title: How We Built an Efficient Contingent Data Management Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

In many organizations using SAP SuccessFactors HCM, contingent workers play a critical role in supporting day-to-day operations and project-based work. But managing this workforce isn’t always as simple as it sounds.

From what I’ve seen, things start to get complicated when the number of contingent workers begins to grow.

HR teams often find themselves handling updates manually — whether it’s hiring new contingent workers, extending contracts, processing transfers, or updating work orders. Each action might seem small on its own, but when multiplied across dozens or even hundreds of workers, it quickly becomes time-consuming and difficult to manage.

I’ve noticed that a lot of this work still depends heavily on spreadsheets, repeated data entry, and constant back-and-forth validations. Contract extensions get delayed, transfers take longer than expected, and even a small mistake in updating a record can lead to inconsistencies in the system.

What really stood out to me was how reactive the process becomes. Instead of having a structured way to manage contingent workforce data, teams are often rushing to keep everything up to date — especially during peak periods or project ramp-ups.

At the same time, scaling this process isn’t easy. As organizations rely more on flexible workforce models, the need to manage large volumes of contingent workers increases. But without the right tools, it becomes harder to maintain speed, accuracy, and control.
